shrine: Ise Jingū — Naikū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine)

events.name: Grand Harvest Offering Festival (神嘗祭, Kannamesai)
events.time: October 15–25; core ceremonies at the Naikū on the nights of October 16–17
events.origin: The Kannamesai is the oldest and most sacred of all Ise Jingū's annual rites, rooted in the mythological moment recorded in the Nihon Shoki when Amaterasu Ōmikami gave her divine grandson Ninigi no Mikoto the sacred rice from her heavenly paddy field (斎庭, yūniwa) as the source of life for the Japanese people. Every autumn since antiquity, the first new grain of the harvest is returned to the goddess before any human being tastes it — a re-enactment of that original divine gift. The Jingū's own official records describe the entire annual calendar of approximately 1,500 rites as revolving around this single ceremony.
events.meaning: The Kannamesai stands at the cosmological center of the entire Jingū. It embodies a sacred covenant: rice is not a human achievement but a divine gift, and every harvest must be offered back to its source before humanity may receive it. The Emperor's own rice seedlings, grown in the Imperial Palace gardens, are presented here alongside offerings from farmers across Japan — a vision of sovereign and commoner joined in a single act of gratitude before the goddess.
events.ritual: The sequence opens on October 15 with the Okitama no Kami-sai (興玉神祭), honoring the resident earth deity, followed by the Mi-ura (御卜) divination in which priests confirm their ritual purity. The Gekū is served first per the ancient custom of Gekū-senzai (外宮先祭). At the Naikū, on the night of October 16 at 10 pm in complete sacred darkness, the Yuki-no-Yūbe-no-Ōmiketachi (由貴夕大御饌) is performed: earthenware vessels filled with steamed unhulled new-harvest rice, freshly pounded mochi, white sake (白酒, shiroki) and black sake (黒酒, kuroki) brewed from the Jingū's own sacred paddy, along with offerings of sea, mountain, river, and field. At 2 am the rite repeats as the Yuki-no-Ashita-no-Ōmiketachi (由貴朝大御饌). At noon on October 17 the Imperial envoy (勅使, chokuji) presents the Emperor's silk offerings in five sacred colors at the Hōhei (奉幣) ceremony. The day closes with the Mikagura (御神楽), a court music performance to soothe and delight the goddess. The inner precincts are adorned with rice sheaves — the Emperor's first fruits (御初穂, mihatsuho) and the Kakechikara (懸税), donated by farming households across Japan.
events.prayer: Participants pray for the continued blessing of the harvest, the flourishing of the imperial line, the prosperity of the nation (国家の隆昌), and the peace and safety of all Japanese people (国民の平安). Pilgrims traveling to Ise during this period offer personal gratitude and ask that the divine bounty continue in the year ahead.

type.notes: The inner rites are closed, but the solemn procession of priests in ancient court dress along the sacred approach path (参道) can be glimpsed from a distance. The Mikagura court music performance on the evening of October 17 adds further atmosphere. More importantly, this is the most spiritually charged moment of the entire year at Ise — arriving on October 15–17 means walking the gravel path and crossing the Uji Bridge at the exact moment the goddess is being honored with the nation's first harvest. Arrive early morning for the quietest and most atmospheric experience before crowds build.

events.name: Grand Shrine Rebuilding — Transfer of the Divine (式年遷宮, Shikinen Sengū)
events.time: Every 20 years. The 63rd cycle began in 2025; the central Transfer ceremony (遷御の儀, Sengyo no Gi) is expected in autumn 2033. The public Okihiki timber-hauling festival will occur in the years approaching 2033.
events.origin: The Shikinen Sengū originated as the sacred wish of Emperor Tenmu (天武天皇), realized by his successor Empress Jitō (持統天皇) in 690 CE as the first ceremony of its kind. It was born from the theological conviction that a goddess of Amaterasu's stature deserves to live not in a monument that slowly decays, but in a home that is always fresh, always new, always worthy of her. The shrine buildings are constructed in the ancient Shinmei-zukuri style (神明造り) — raised cypress wood with thatched roofs — deliberately designed to be rebuilt rather than preserved. After one prolonged interruption during the civil wars of the Sengoku period, the rite has continued unbroken, completing 62 full cycles as of 2013.
events.meaning: The Sengū embodies the concept of Tokowaka (常若) — 'eternally young.' Its philosophical paradox is that by destroying what exists and building anew, the ancient is preserved not as a relic but as a living reality. The shrine is simultaneously 1,300 years old and brand new. The 20-year cycle is also the single most important mechanism for transmitting irreplaceable craft traditions across generations — the master carpentry of the miyaiku (宮大工), sacred silk weaving, and the forging of 714 types of divine treasures (神宝, shinpō) in 1,576 items using Nara and Heian period techniques. The Jingū has even been growing its own cypress forest since the Taishō era with the plan that by the 22nd century the sacred building timber will be entirely self-sufficient.
events.ritual: The Sengū unfolds across approximately 30 distinct ceremonies over eight years. It begins with the Yamaguchi-sai (山口祭), asking the mountain deity's permission to harvest timber in the sacred forest. The most joyful public event is the Okihiki Gyōji (御木曳行事): the entire community of Ise participates in hauling great cypress logs — by river at the Naikū, by cart at the Gekū — with traditional costumes, work songs, and the thunderous communal call of 'Enya!' The climax is the Sengyo no Gi (遷御の儀), performed in absolute darkness on an autumn night: the sacred mirror of Amaterasu (the Yata no Kagami, 八咫鏡) is carried in a torchlit procession to the newly built shrine, attendants holding white silk cloth to shield the divine from impure sight. The ancient timber is never discarded — the great ridge columns of the old Main Hall become the torii gate of the Uji Bridge, then 20 years later pass to roadside gates, completing a 60-year cycle of sacred service.
events.prayer: The Sengū is a prayer that spans generations: those who plant the trees will never see them used; those who harvest the timber never planted it. It is a communal supplication that the bond between the Japanese people and their gods will continue unbroken into a future no one alive today can see — praying for the eternal vitality of the goddess, the preservation of irreplaceable cultural heritage, and the peace and prosperity of the nation for the next 20 years and beyond.

type.notes: The Okihiki Gyōji timber-hauling procession is fully public, festive, and one of the most extraordinary living folk traditions in Japan — an entire city pulling sacred logs together in ancient costume. The final Transfer ceremony (Sengyo no Gi) is closed and performed in darkness, but the years approaching 2033 will generate significant public events. If you can visit Ise during the Okihiki years (likely 2031–2032), do not miss it. This is a once-in-a-generation opportunity that will not come again until 2053.

events.name: Prayer for the Spring Harvest (祈年祭, Kinensai)
events.time: February 17–23; main rites at the Naikū on February 17 (Ōmiketachi at 11 am, Hōhei at 2 pm)
events.origin: The Kinensai, also read as 'Toshigoi no Matsuri' (としごいのまつり), is one of Japan's oldest national rites. In its name, 'toshi' (年) was an archaic poetic word for rice, and 'koi' (乞い) means to beseech — making it literally a supplication addressed to the gods of grain. According to the Engishiki (延喜式), in the Heian period this rite was observed simultaneously at 2,861 shrines across the nation, with the Emperor dispatching an Imperial envoy (勅使) to Ise as the supreme act. It opens the sacred agricultural cycle that will not close until the Niinamesai in November — together they form the full arc of rice civilization as understood by ancient Japan.
events.meaning: The Kinensai marks the threshold between the dormant world and the active one — the moment when farmers commit their faith and labor to the earth and ask the gods to honor that commitment. It reflects the ancient belief that agriculture is not merely a human endeavor but a collaboration between humans and deities, and that without divine consent expressed through a good harvest, civilization itself cannot continue. At Ise specifically, it carries the additional weight of the myth of Ninigi and the heavenly paddy: every spring planting is a re-enactment of the first seeds ever sown on earth.
events.ritual: The central rites are the Ōmiketachi (大御饌), in which food offerings are prepared and presented to Amaterasu and all other enshrined deities, followed by the Hōhei (奉幣), in which the Imperial envoy presents the Emperor's silk offerings. Both rites proceed first at the Gekū and then at the Naikū, and the festival extends outward to all subsidiary shrines through February 23. Preparatory purification (大祓, Ōharae) is performed on January 31 to cleanse all officiating priests before the ceremonies begin.
events.prayer: Participants beseech Amaterasu and the other gods for the abundant ripening of the five grains (五穀豊穣, gokoku hōjō), the safety of those who work the fields, and by extension the peace and stability of the entire nation whose survival depends on the harvest.

type.notes: No public ceremony or performance to watch — the rites happen entirely within the closed inner precinct. The value for a visitor is in the timing: arriving on February 17 means walking the quiet, uncrowded approach path on the day the goddess is being asked to bless the coming year's harvest. February brings early plum blossoms to the surrounding area, cold clean air, and almost no tourist crowds — the shrine feels most itself in this stillness. Come to reflect, not to watch.

events.name: Harvest Thanksgiving Festival (新嘗祭, Niinamesai)
events.time: November 23–29; main rites at the Naikū on November 23 (Ōmiketachi at 11 am, Hōhei at 2 pm)
events.origin: The Niinamesai — also read Shinjōsai — is the harvest thanksgiving counterpart that closes the sacred agricultural arc opened by the Kinensai in February. Its name means 'tasting the new grain': 'nii' (新) means new and 'name' (嘗) means to partake. At the Imperial Palace on this same date, the Emperor personally cultivates rice and becomes the first human being to taste the new harvest — sharing it with the gods in a moment of direct divine-imperial communion. This rite is now a national public holiday (勤労感謝の日). At Ise specifically, the Niinamesai was not traditionally observed as a separate rite — because the Kannamesai in October already presents new grain to Amaterasu — and was only added in Meiji 5 (1872) when the new government standardized imperial ritual across all major shrines.
events.meaning: The Niinamesai closes the cosmological loop that the Kinensai opens. If the spring festival asks the gods to make the harvest possible, the autumn festival answers: it happened, and we are grateful. Together, Kinensai and Niinamesai frame the entire human relationship with the land as a sacred dialogue — a question asked in February and answered in November. The act of the Emperor personally eating the new grain alongside the gods collapses the distance between the divine and human worlds into a single shared meal.
events.ritual: The ceremony mirrors the structure of the Kinensai: the Ōmiketachi offering of food is performed first at the Gekū, then at the Naikū, followed by the Hōhei in which the Imperial envoy presents the Emperor's offerings. Rites then cascade outward to all subordinate shrines through November 29. The preparatory Ōharae purification is performed on October 31.
events.prayer: Those who observe the Niinamesai give thanks for the harvest that has been granted and pray that the gods will accept this expression of gratitude — renewing the bond of trust between the divine and human worlds so that the cycle may begin again in spring. Pilgrims pray for the safety of their families through the coming winter and for the continued blessing of the land.

type.notes: Like the Kinensai, the rites are entirely closed to the public. The pilgrimage value is in arriving on November 23 — the day the Emperor himself shares a meal with the gods — and walking the ancient approach path in the knowledge that the entire harvest cycle of the year is being closed in the sanctuary before you. Late November brings autumn foliage to the cedar forests around the Isuzu River, making this one of the most visually beautiful times to visit. Come in the morning before the afternoon crowds arrive for the national holiday.

shrine: Ise Jingū — Gekū (豊受大神宮, Outer Shrine)

events.name: Grand Harvest Offering Festival (神嘗祭, Kannamesai)
events.time: October 15–25; core ceremonies at the Gekū on the nights of October 15–16, one day ahead of the Naikū. Per the ancient rule of Gekū-senzai (外宮先祭), the Outer Shrine is always served first.
events.origin: The ritual structure and national significance of the Kannamesai are the same as at the Naikū — the year's first harvest is offered to the gods before any human tastes it, and the entire Jingū calendar revolves around this ceremony. However, the founding story at the Gekū is distinct. According to the Toyouke Kōtaijin Goshinkei Hongi (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀), Amaterasu herself appeared in the dream of Emperor Yūryaku (雄略天皇) and said: 'I cannot eat my meals in peace alone — summon Toyouke Ōmikami from Tamba Province to serve at my side.' The Emperor obeyed, and Toyouke was enshrined at Yamada-no-hara in Ise around the 22nd year of his reign. The Kannamesai at the Gekū is therefore not simply about offering new grain to a deity — it is about fulfilling Toyouke's eternal divine function: preparing and presenting the sacred meal to Amaterasu.
events.meaning: Where the Naikū's Kannamesai centers on Amaterasu as the giver of rice to humanity, the Gekū's ceremony centers on Toyouke as the divine intermediary who transforms the harvest into sacred nourishment. Toyouke is the goddess of food (食), clothing (衣), and shelter (住) — the three material foundations of civilization. The Kannamesai at the Gekū acknowledges that even the sun goddess cannot sustain herself or bless the world alone; she requires a companion deity devoted entirely to the act of giving and preparing. This is a cosmological statement about the nature of sustenance itself: abundance must be tended, prepared, and offered with devotion before it becomes truly nourishing.
events.ritual: The sequence and structure mirror the Naikū exactly — Okitama no Kami-sai (興玉神祭), Mi-ura divination (御卜), Yuki-no-Yūbe-no-Ōmiketachi (由貴夕大御饌) at 10 pm, Yuki-no-Ashita-no-Ōmiketachi (由貴朝大御饌) at 2 am, Hōhei (奉幣) with the Imperial envoy at noon, and the evening Mikagura (御神楽). The key difference is timing: all of these occur one day earlier than at the Naikū, as the Gekū must be honored before the Naikū may begin.
events.prayer: Prayers at the Gekū Kannamesai share the same four great intentions as the Naikū — the flourishing of the imperial line, abundant harvest, national prosperity, and the peace of all people — but are addressed through Toyouke, the goddess whose divine office is to ensure that the material blessings of the earth are properly prepared and delivered. Pilgrims with connections to food, agriculture, clothing, or any form of industry find particular resonance in praying here.

type.notes: Same experience as the Naikū — priestly processions in ancient court dress can be glimpsed from the approach path (参道), and the Mikagura performance adds atmosphere on the evening of October 16. Because the Gekū ceremonies fall one day before the Naikū, visiting October 15–16 at the Gekū and then October 16–17 at the Naikū allows a dedicated pilgrim to follow the full ceremonial sequence across both shrines in its correct ancient order.

events.name: Grand Shrine Rebuilding — Transfer of the Divine (式年遷宮, Shikinen Sengū)
events.time: Every 20 years, same cycle as the Naikū. The 63rd cycle began in 2025; the Sengyo no Gi (遷御の儀) is expected in autumn 2033.
events.origin: The origin, founding history, and philosophical meaning of the Shikinen Sengū are identical to those described for the Naikū — initiated by Emperor Tenmu, first performed in 690 CE, embodying the concept of Tokowaka (常若, eternal youth) through perpetual renewal. Both shrines are rebuilt simultaneously on the same 20-year cycle.
events.meaning: Same as the Naikū: the paradox of preserving the ancient by rebuilding it anew, transmitting irreplaceable craft traditions across generations, and sustaining the living covenant between the divine and human worlds. The Gekū's rebuilding carries the additional layer that Toyouke's shrine — the divine kitchen, the source of the sacred meal — must also always be fresh and worthy, because a goddess whose purpose is to nourish cannot be housed in something decayed.
events.ritual: The Sengū rites at the Gekū follow the same approximately 30-ceremony sequence as the Naikū. The most significant difference is in the Okihiki Gyōji (御木曳行事): while the Naikū hauls its sacred cypress timber along the Isuzu River by boat, the Gekū transports its logs entirely by land on large ceremonial carts (奉曳車, hōeisha) pulled through the streets of Ise — a distinction that reflects the different geographical settings of the two shrines and gives the Gekū's Okihiki a distinctly urban, street-festival character. Both processions share the same joyful community spirit, traditional costumes, and the communal cry of 'Enya!'
events.prayer: Same generational prayer as the Naikū — for the eternal continuity of the divine-human covenant, the preservation of irreplaceable heritage, and the peace of the nation for the next 20 years and beyond.

type.notes: The Gekū's Okihiki land procession through the streets of Ise is arguably more accessible and visually dramatic for tourists than the Naikū's river-based equivalent, as it passes directly through the town. Watch for announcements from the official Jingū website as 2033 approaches. Next occurrence after 2033 will not come until 2053.

events.name: Prayer for the Spring Harvest (祈年祭, Kinensai)
events.time: February 17; main rites at the Gekū begin earlier than the Naikū — Ōmiketachi at 4 am, Hōhei at 7 am — then the Naikū follows at 11 am and 2 pm respectively.
events.origin: The national history and ancient legal basis of the Kinensai are the same as at the Naikū — one of Japan's oldest rites, observed simultaneously at 2,861 shrines in the Heian period, rooted in the myth of Ninigi and the heavenly rice paddy. At the Gekū specifically, the prayer takes on an additional dimension: Toyouke Ōmikami was summoned to Ise precisely because the act of preparing and offering food is itself a divine responsibility. To pray for the harvest at the Gekū is to petition not just any deity but the goddess whose entire existence is defined by the transformation of grain into sacred sustenance.
events.meaning: The Kinensai at the Gekū addresses Toyouke as the divine patron of all those who grow, prepare, and provide food — farmers, fishermen, weavers, craftspeople. Where the Naikū frames the spring prayer in terms of Amaterasu's cosmic gift of rice to humanity, the Gekū frames it in terms of the labor and craft required to honor that gift: the planting, the tending, the harvesting, and the cooking. It is a prayer that dignifies human work as a form of sacred participation.
events.ritual: Identical in structure to the Naikū — Ōmiketachi offering followed by Hōhei with the Imperial envoy — but performed earlier in the day, as the Gekū must precede the Naikū. The rites then extend outward to all Gekū subsidiary shrines through February 23.
events.prayer: Participants pray for abundant crops (五穀豊穣, gokoku hōjō), the safety and prosperity of all those who work in food, agriculture, and industry, and through Toyouke's specific divine domain, for the blessing of clothing and shelter as well. The Gekū is considered particularly appropriate for prayers related to one's livelihood and daily sustenance.

type.notes: Same contemplative, closed-ceremony experience as the Naikū on February 17. The Gekū is notably less crowded than the Naikū at all times of year, making the pilgrimage atmosphere here quieter and more intimate. For visitors whose prayers relate to work, food, or material livelihood, the Gekū Kinensai carries a more personally resonant spiritual address than the Naikū.

events.name: Harvest Thanksgiving Festival (新嘗祭, Niinamesai)
events.time: November 23; main rites at the Gekū begin earlier — Ōmiketachi at 4 am, Hōhei at 7 am — preceding the Naikū's 11 am and 2 pm ceremonies.
events.origin: The Niinamesai's national history, imperial significance, and the note that it was only added to the Jingū in Meiji 5 (1872) are the same as described for the Naikū. The particular resonance at the Gekū is this: if Toyouke was summoned by Amaterasu to ensure she would never eat alone, then the Niinamesai — the moment when the new harvest is finally tasted and shared — is the fulfillment of Toyouke's founding divine mission. The harvest thanksgiving at the Gekū is the completion of the story that began with Emperor Yūryaku's dream.
events.meaning: The Niinamesai at the Gekū closes the sacred arc begun by the Kinensai, but through the lens of Toyouke's identity as the divine provider: the harvest is not merely acknowledged but celebrated as the successful outcome of the collaboration between the sun goddess who gave rice to humanity and the food goddess who ensures it is properly tended and offered. It is the moment when Toyouke's eternal service — cooking the divine meal, day after day for 1,500 years — is honored and seen.
events.ritual: Identical in structure to the Naikū — Ōmiketachi followed by Hōhei with the Imperial envoy — performed earlier in the day per the Gekū-senzai rule. Rites extend to all subsidiary Gekū shrines through November 29.
events.prayer: Those who pray at the Gekū Niinamesai give thanks for the material blessings of the year — food, clothing, livelihood, shelter — and ask that Toyouke continue to sustain these foundations of daily life through the coming winter and into the new year. It is an especially meaningful time for those whose work involves feeding, making, or providing for others.

type.notes: Same closed-ceremony pilgrimage experience as the Naikū on November 23. The Gekū's cedar forest and Magatama Pond (勾玉池) are particularly beautiful in late autumn foliage. Because the Gekū is always less crowded than the Naikū — especially notable on a national holiday like November 23 — this is the more peaceful of the two shrines to visit for a quiet, reflective close to the harvest year.

deities: Homuda Wake no Mikoto (誉田別尊) — Emperor Ōjin, the god of war and archery
Okinaga Tarashi Hime no Mikoto (息長足姫尊) — Empress Jingū, mother of Emperor Ōjin
Hime-gami (比賣神) — the attendant goddess of Hachiman

events.name: Sagicho Festival — The Float Fire Burning (左義長祭, Sagicho-sai)
events.time: The Saturday and Sunday nearest to March 14–15 each year. Rain or shine — never cancelled. In 2027: March 13–14.
events.origin: The Sagicho is a fire purification rite of Chinese origin, introduced to Japan during the reign of Emperor Ninmyō (仁明天皇, r. 833–850) as a ceremony to pray for the protection of the nation and the abundance of the five grains. For centuries it was a January court rite. The specific and extraordinary form it takes at Himure Hachimangū traces directly to one man: Oda Nobunaga. When Nobunaga built his castle at nearby Azuchi, he held lavish Sagicho fire festivals every New Year, and historical records in the Shinkōki (信長記) describe him personally dancing in the procession wearing a Southern Barbarian hat, his face wrapped in red silk, dressed in an embroidered robe — extravagant, theatrical, deliberately shocking. After Nobunaga's death, his nephew Toyotomi Hidetsugu built Hachiman Castle on Mount Hachiman and settled merchants from Azuchi into the new castle town below. These new townspeople, astonished by the solemnity and grandeur of the existing Hachiman Festival (八幡祭) already celebrated by the older villages, wanted their own festival to rival it. They chose Sagicho, channeling the memory of Nobunaga's legendary revelry, and dedicated it to the shrine as an offering of joy at the town's founding and as a prayer for protection from fire and evil.
events.meaning: The Sagicho at Himure Hachimangū is a festival of creative pride, communal rivalry, and purification through fire. The elaborate floats (だし, dashi) — constructed entirely from natural food materials, grains, and local products shaped into the year's zodiac animal — represent each neighborhood's identity, craftsmanship, and wit. The competition is fierce and proud: neighborhoods invest enormous effort and keep their designs secret until the day of revelation. When the floats are finally burned at the festival's climax, all that artistry is willingly surrendered to fire — an act of purification and offering that transforms human creativity into sacred smoke ascending to the gods. It is simultaneously a celebration of abundance (the food materials used) and a release of attachment.
events.ritual: The festival spans three days. On the preceding Friday afternoon, the Mikuji-sai (御鬮祭) — a sacred lottery — determines the order in which each neighborhood's float will be burned, a moment of great suspense. On Saturday, the floats — three-meter-tall triangular pine torches decorated with twelve tiers of fresh straw, topped with bamboo, red paper streamers, and fans — are carried on poles by the Odoriko (踊子, festival dancers) who wear matching half-coats (踊半纏), elaborate makeup, and red-and-white sandals, chanting 'Chōsa, Yare Yare!' as they dance and sway under the weight. The floats process through the old merchant quarter of the city in a winding procession (渡御, togyo), then gather at the shrine for the Dashi Contest (左義長だしコンクール) judging and awards ceremony. On Sunday, after the main shrine rites in the morning, the floats freely roam the narrow old streets in cheerful, raucous fashion — bumping into one another in the beloved Kenka (ケンカ, 'fight') tradition, where neighborhoods deliberately collide their floats as a playful show of spirit. At dusk, the floats re-enter the shrine grounds. Five floats are set alight simultaneously at 8 pm, and the rest follow in lottery order until nearly 11 pm, the entire horse ground (馬場) becoming a sea of flame against the early spring night sky.
events.prayer: Participants pray primarily for yakuyoke (厄除け) — the warding off of evil and misfortune — and hibuse (火防) — protection from fire, the greatest danger to the old wooden merchant town. The burning of the floats is an act of sending those prayers upward in smoke. Neighborhoods also pray for the prosperity of their businesses, the health of their families, and community pride. For the Ōmihachiman merchants who descend from the great Ōmi trading families, the Sagicho has always been an expression of commercial confidence and civic gratitude.

type.notes: One of the most visually spectacular and accessible festivals in the Kinki region. Everything is public: the Saturday procession through the old merchant quarter (八幡堀 area) is lined with spectators and gives an extraordinary view of the elaborate food-art floats up close. The Sunday burning from 8 pm is the unmissable climax — arrive by 7 pm to secure a good position in the shrine grounds. The combination of enormous bonfires, chanting crowds, and the beautiful backdrop of the old townscape makes this one of the most photogenic night festivals in Japan. Designated National Intangible Folk Cultural Property.

events.name: Hachiman Festival — The Great Fire and Drum Festival (八幡祭, Hachiman-sai)
events.time: April 14 (Yoimiya / Eve Festival — Torch Night), April 15 (Honsai / Main Festival — Drum Day), April 16 (Sudera Watari — Procession to the Shrine's Outer Resting Place). Core events: torches lit from 8 pm on April 14; drum procession from 4 pm on April 15.
events.origin: The Hachiman Festival is the 例祭 (reisai) — the grand annual festival — of Himure Hachimangū, and its origin reaches back to one of the most ancient imperial legends connected to this land. According to shrine tradition, in the 6th year of Emperor Ōjin's reign (275 CE), the Emperor traveled by boat across Lake Biwa to visit the birthplace of his mother, Empress Jingū, in the village of Okinaga (modern Maibara City). When he came ashore at Minamitsuda — now the Tsuda reclaimed land — seven households living along the lakeshore gathered reeds and rushes from the water's edge, fashioned them into torches, and lit them to guide the Emperor through the darkness to Hachiman. This act of humble, devoted light-bearing — commoners illuminating the path of a god-descended Emperor using the simplest materials at hand — became the founding myth of the festival. Those seven households' torches have multiplied, across nearly 1,750 years, into the great fire procession of today.
events.meaning: The Hachiman Festival is about loyalty, community, and the endurance of ancestral bonds across time. The twelve villages (十二郷, jūni-gō) that serve the festival — Ichii, Taga, Kitanoshō, Takakai, Nakamura, Utsuro, Ōbayashi, Tsuchida, Funaki, Kobunaki, Ōfusa, Minamitsuda — are the same communities whose ancestors lit those first torches for Emperor Ōjin. Their neighborhood names survive to this day as the street names of Ōmihachiman. Each郷's participation is therefore not merely tradition but a living genealogical connection to the founding moment: to carry a drum or a torch in this festival is to embody the unbroken chain of one's ancestors performing the same act. The Shuushi (シューシ, 崇祀) — the moment when young men hoist the enormous drums high and strike them three times before the deity — is understood as an act that magnifies the divine power of Hachiman himself.
events.ritual: On April 14, from 8 am, the great Sasachimatsu (笹松明) — two towering pine torches woven from bamboo and rushes, the divine seats of the gods — are assembled in the shrine's horse ground (馬場). In the afternoon, children light smaller torches in a dedicated ceremony. At 6 pm, each of the twelve villages processes into the shrine grounds with their drums and torches; the Yoimiya-sai (宵宮祭) begins at 6:30 pm. At 8 pm, fireworks ignited by each village signal the start: torches are lit one by one according to ancient precedence — dragging torches, swinging torches, standing torches — in a precisely ordered sequence unchanged for centuries. Towers of flame and the thunder of drums fill the night until past 11 pm. The evening closes with two sacred inner rites: the Maijara (まいじゃら), performed before the golden portable shrine (神輿) in the worship hall, and the Kezurikake (けずりかけ), performed before the main sanctuary — the thousand-year spear (千年鉾) is then offered before the hall, formally opening the drum day. On April 15, the morning begins with the formal Reisai (例祭) inside the main sanctuary, receiving the envoy of the Association of Shinto Shrines. From 4 pm, the twelve villages gather at their designated lodges, and the great procession begins: young men shoulder drums weighing several hundred kan (hundreds of kilograms), marching under the cry of 'Dokkoishan-se!' When each village's procession reaches the front of the worship hall, the young men hoist the drum high above their heads and strike it powerfully three times — the Shuushi (崇祀) — in a single synchronized offering of strength and devotion to Hachiman. On April 16, the festival continues with the Sudera Watari (須寺御旅所お渡り) — a procession to the portable shrine's temporary resting place — followed by the return procession and closing rites.
events.prayer: Participants pray for the protection of the community (鎮護国家, chingo kokka), the flourishing of business and trade — historically the prayer of the great Ōmi merchants who carried Hachiman's spirit with them on their trading routes across Japan and overseas — and for the health and longevity of their families. The Shuushi drum offering is specifically a prayer to amplify Hachiman's divine power and invite his blessing upon the village that performs it. The April 16 Enrei-juju-sai (還暦・寿賀延寿祭) also makes this a day when those reaching milestone ages pray for longevity.

type.notes: Both nights are fully public and extraordinary. April 14 from 8 pm is the more dramatic — over 200 torches of various sizes, cascading fireworks from each village, and the enormous Sasachimatsu blazing against the night sky, all accompanied by the unceasing roll of drums. April 15 from 4 pm is more solemn and processional — the sight of young men carrying drums the size of small cars on their shoulders through the shrine approach is genuinely awe-inspiring. The Shuushi moment — drums raised and struck three times at the worship hall — is the emotional and spiritual peak of the entire festival. Designated National Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Arrive early for a good viewing position along the horse ground; the area fills quickly after 7 pm.

events.name: Ten Thousand Lantern Festival (萬燈祭, Mantōsai)
events.time: August 15 and 16, both evenings. Lanterns lit at approximately 6:30 pm; closing ceremony at 9 pm.
events.origin: The Mantōsai is held on the two evenings of Obon (お盆) — the Buddhist and Shinto period when the spirits of ancestors are believed to return to the world of the living. The tradition of lighting lanterns (御燈明, miakashi) during Obon is one of the most ancient in Japanese religious life, rooted in the belief that the light guides the souls of the departed to find their way home and back again. At Himure Hachimangū, devotees and those who have received the shrine's blessing dedicate lanterns in gratitude for the divine grace (御神恩, goshinon) received during the year, transforming the shrine's forecourt into a sea of glowing light as an offering of thanks from the living to the gods and in memory of the dead.
events.meaning: Where the Sagicho and Hachiman festivals are communal spectacles of fire and motion, the Mantōsai is their opposite: still, warm, and intimate. It is the moment when the shrine turns inward — when individual gratitude, grief, and remembrance replace collective celebration. The thousands of lanterns, each dedicated by a specific person or family, make the darkness of summer alive with personal meaning. The Obon context adds the layer of ancestral connection: the living and the dead are understood to be temporarily reunited under the same lantern light, sharing the same sacred space before the deity of Hachiman.
events.ritual: On both evenings, thousands of dedicated lanterns are arranged across the wide forecourt of the shrine (御神前の広庭). At 6:30 pm the lanterns are lit, gradually transforming the grounds into a luminous field. At 7 pm, a formal Hōkoku-sai (奉告祭, notification ceremony) is performed before the deity. On the 15th, the ceremony is followed by a dedicated performance of wadaiko (和太鼓, Japanese drumming); on the 16th, by Bon-odori (盆踊り, the traditional circle dance performed during Obon to welcome and honor returning ancestors). At 9 pm the lanterns are extinguished.
events.prayer: Participants pray in gratitude for the blessings and protections received from Hachiman during the year, and in remembrance of their ancestors and the departed. Lantern dedication is an act of giving light — offering visibility and warmth to the gods and to the souls of those no longer living. It is one of the purest expressions of on-gaeshi (恩返し), the Japanese concept of returning grace received.

type.notes: The Mantōsai is the most contemplative and personally meaningful event in the shrine's calendar — a genuine moment of stillness amid a year dominated by fire and spectacle. There is no competition, no procession, no performance to watch in the conventional sense. Visitors come to dedicate a lantern, sit in the warm August darkness surrounded by thousands of other flames, and simply be present. The combination of Obon atmosphere, summer heat, lantern light, and the shrine's beautiful wooded setting makes this one of the most quietly moving evenings Ōmihachiman offers. Far fewer visitors than the spring fire festivals — arrive after 7 pm and stay until the Bon-odori ends on the 16th for the fullest experience.

shrine: Yasaka Shrine (八坂神社) — also known as Gion-sha (祇園社)
deities: Susanoo no Mikoto (素戔嗚尊) — the storm god, purifier of all calamity, principal deity
Kushiinadahime no Mikoto (櫛稲田姫命) — wife of Susanoo, goddess of rice and the harvest
Yahashira no Mikogami (八柱御子神) — the eight divine children of Susanoo and Kushiinadahime

events.name: Gion Festival (祇園祭, Gion Matsuri)
events.time: July 1–31, the entire month. Core public events: Yamaboko Junko front procession (前祭山鉾巡行) on July 17; Shinkosai portable shrine procession (神幸祭) on the evening of July 17; Yamaboko Junko rear procession (後祭山鉾巡行) and Hanagasa Junko procession (花傘巡行) on July 24; Kankosai (還幸祭) return procession on the evening of July 24; closing rite (疫神社夏越祭) on July 31.
events.origin: The Gion Matsuri is the oldest and most historically significant urban festival in Japan, with a recorded origin in the Jōgan era. In 869 CE, a devastating epidemic swept Kyoto and spread across the country. Acting on imperial decree, 66 sacred spears — one for each province of Japan — were erected, and the portable shrine (神輿, mikoshi) of Gion-sha was carried in solemn procession to Shinsen-en (神泉苑), the imperial garden pond at the heart of the capital, to pray for the removal of pestilence and calamity. This rite was called the Gion Goryōe (祇園御霊会), the 'Gion Spirit-Pacification Assembly.' The number 66 was not arbitrary — it was a declaration that the entire nation, every corner of the realm, was implicated in this plague and that this single ritual act was being performed on behalf of all of Japan simultaneously. The festival expanded over subsequent centuries: by the mid-Heian period, floats, dengaku music, and sarugaku performance had been added. The Ōnin War (1467) reduced Kyoto to ash and the festival to silence, but the townspeople (町衆, machishū) revived it in 1500 and introduced the lottery system (鬮取式, kujitori-shiki) to determine float procession order. With each revival from fire and war, the machishū invested more — more elaborate float decorations, more exquisite tapestries — until the floats became known as 'moving art museums of the Orient', hung with 16th-century Flemish tapestries, Chinese silks, and Indian textiles acquired through Japan's maritime trade routes.
events.meaning: The Gion Matsuri is, at its theological core, an act of placation and purification: the god Susanoo, who is himself the deity of storms, calamity, and the unknown forces that bring pestilence, is paraded through the city in his portable shrines so that his divine power cleanses every street he passes through. The mountain-and-spear floats (山鉾, yamaboko) that precede him are not decorative — they are yorishiro (依り代), objects designed to attract, absorb, and contain the plague spirits (疫神, ekijin) and wandering vengeful souls (怨霊, onryō) that cause disease. The gion-bayashi music — the hypnotic 'kon-chiki-chin' of gong, drum, and flute — is specifically structured to soothe wild spirits into submission. The festival is therefore a cosmic act of urban sanitation: before the god can travel safely through the city, the city must first be spiritually cleansed by the floats that go before him. At a deeper level, the Gion Matsuri embodies the foundational belief of the Gion faith: that Susanoo, the most dangerous and unpredictable of the great gods, is also the most powerful protector — if properly honoured.
events.ritual: The festival unfolds across the entire month of July in a precisely choreographed sequence. July 1 (Kisshu-iri, 吉符入): the child ritual attendant (稚児, chigo) of the lead float, Naginata-boko, makes a formal pilgrimage to the shrine — from this day he is elevated to the status of a divine being, must not touch the ground, and is carried everywhere on the shoulders of attendants. July 2: the lottery-draw ceremony (鬮取式) at Kyoto City Hall determines the procession order for the 32 floats. July 10: the Mikoshi Arai-shiki (神輿洗式) — a torch-lit procession escorts the Nakamikoza portable shrine (carrying Susanoo's spirit) down to the Kamo River at Shijo Bridge, where sacred water drawn that morning is used to ritually cleanse the shrine. July 13: the Naginata-boko chigo makes his formal horse-mounted procession to Yasaka Shrine for the 'rank-receiving' ceremony (位もらい), in which he is invested with the court rank of fifth-rank lieutenant and the standing of a 10,000-koku lord. July 14–16 (Yoiyama, 宵山): the evenings before the main procession — floats are illuminated in their home neighborhoods, Gion hayashi music fills the air, the old Kyoto merchant townhouses open their screens to display ancient treasures and folding screens (屏風飾り), and the streets near Shijo and Karasuma become pedestrian-only rivers of lantern light. July 17 (Yamaboko Junko front parade, 前祭): from 9 am, 23 floats process from Shijo-Karasuma through Shijo-Kawaramachi and along Oike Avenue — the Naginata-boko leads, its chigo cutting the sacred straw rope (注連縄) stretched across the road with a single sword strike. That evening, the three portable shrines of Yasaka depart from the stone steps, pause at the bottom for the dramatic 'lifting ceremony' (差し上げ), and process through the eastern city to the Shijo Otabisho (四条御旅所), Susanoo's temporary resting place. They remain there for seven days. July 24 (Yamaboko Junko rear parade, 後祭): 10 floats process in the second parade, restored in 2014 after a 48-year absence, led by the Hashibenkei-yama. Simultaneously, the Hanagasa Junko (花傘巡行) — a colorful procession of parasol floats, mounted child attendants, and geisha dancers — follows a different route and arrives at the shrine for dance performances. That evening, the three portable shrines leave the Otabisho for the return procession (還幸祭, Kankosai), traveling west of Teramachi Avenue before returning to Yasaka in the small hours. July 28: a second Mikoshi Arai-shiki purifies the portable shrines before they are stored. July 31 (Ekijinja Natsu-goshi-sai, 疫神社夏越祭): at the small Ekijinja sub-shrine within the Yasaka grounds — dedicated to Somin Shorai, the poor man who sheltered Susanoo and received his protection — a great straw ring (大茅輪) is erected at the torii gate. Worshippers pass through it to shed all accumulated misfortune of the year, and receive the 'Somin Shorai no Shison Nari' (蘇民将来之子孫也) protective charm. With this rite the festival formally closes.
events.prayer: The Gion Matsuri is fundamentally a prayer for yakuyoke (厄除け) — the removal of evil and misfortune — and for protection from disease, disaster, and the invisible forces of spiritual pollution that accumulate in a city over time. It is also a prayer for the peace and stability of the nation (鎮護国家), echoing its origin as a rite performed on behalf of all 66 provinces. On a personal level, participants receive the Gion Matsuri chimaki (粽) — a bamboo-leaf charm inscribed with 'Somin Shorai no Shison Nari' — which they hang at the entrance of their homes for the year as a pledge of Susanoo's protection. The chigo's role as a divine messenger also channels prayers for children's healthy growth and the safety of young people.

type.notes: The Gion Matsuri is the most famous festival in Japan and one of the greatest public spectacles in Asia. Everything is fully accessible to visitors. The best single day for most tourists is July 16 (evening Yoiyama before the front parade) — the illuminated floats in the narrower streets of the Nishiki and Shijo neighborhoods, combined with the haunting Gion hayashi music echoing off old merchant walls, is the most atmospheric experience the festival offers. July 17 from 9 am for the full procession requires arriving at viewing spots by 7:30 am for a good position. The evening Shinkosai procession on July 17 (departing from the stone steps around 6:30 pm) is powerful and far less crowded than the daytime parade — three golden portable shrines carried on the shoulders of hundreds of bearers under torchlight. July 31 for the closing Natsu-goshi-sai at Ekijinja is deeply moving and almost unknown to tourists — one of the most spiritually resonant moments of the entire month, involving just the small sub-shrine, a giant straw ring, and a handful of quiet worshippers.

events.name: Bean-Throwing Purification Festival (節分祭, Setsubun-sai)
events.time: February 2 and 3 (both days). Morning purification rites from 9 am; public bean-throwing (豆撒き, mamemaki) at the Maidono (舞殿) dance pavilion throughout both days; traditional dance performances (舞踊奉納) by the four Gion geisha districts.
events.origin: Setsubun — the 'division between seasons' — marks the last day of winter by the old lunar calendar and the eve of spring (立春, risshun). Its purification rites are among the oldest in Japan, rooted in the Heian-period court ceremony of Tsuina (追儺), in which demons of pestilence were ritually driven out of the palace with arrows and shouting. The practice of throwing roasted soybeans — 'demons out, good fortune in!' (鬼は外、福は内) — developed from this court rite into a national popular custom. At Yasaka Shrine, the Setsubun-sai carries special weight because Susanoo himself is the deity of purification from pestilence and evil — this is his domain, and the expulsion of winter's demons is one of the most natural expressions of his protective power.
events.meaning: Setsubun at Yasaka is the smallest-scale expression of the same cosmic logic that drives the Gion Matsuri: the accumulated spiritual pollution of the past year — illness, misfortune, and evil influence — must be physically expelled before the new year can begin cleanly. The beans thrown at demons are not symbolic; in Shinto understanding they carry genuine purifying force, charged by the ritual context. The geisha dances performed at the shrine add a layer of beauty and art as offering to the god — the same principle as the kagura performance at Ise: the divine responds to the gift of skilled, devoted human expression.
events.ritual: On both February 2 and 3, purification rites are held in the main sanctuary from 9 am. At the Maidono open-air dance pavilion, the four geisha districts of the Gion area — Gion Higashi, Gion Kobu, Miyagawa-cho, and Kamishichiken — take turns performing traditional Kyoto dance (舞踊奉納) as sacred offerings, dressed in full apprentice (舞妓, maiko) and geisha (芸妓, geiko) costume. This is one of the very few times in the year when the Gion geisha world appears in an unmediated public sacred context — not at a tea house, not at a performance venue, but directly in front of the god. After each dance performance, roasted soybeans and lucky charm sticks (串札, kushifuda) are scattered by performers, shrine officials, and invited dignitaries from the Maidono platform into the assembled crowd below. Special Setsubun-only protective charms are distributed only during these two days.
events.prayer: Participants pray for yakuyoke (厄除け) — the warding off of evil — and for kenkou (健康) and kōfuku (幸福), health and happiness for the coming year. Catching a thrown bean or charm is considered an act of receiving the shrine's direct blessing. Those in a yakudoshi (厄年) — the traditionally inauspicious years of age 25, 42 (men) or 19, 33 (women) — have particular reason to attend, as Setsubun is the most auspicious moment in the year for the formal purification of bad fortune.

type.notes: Highly recommended for tourists — visually beautiful, fully public, and utterly unique to Kyoto. The sight of Gion maiko in full winter kimono performing classical dance at an outdoor shrine pavilion in the cold February air, followed by beans and charms raining down from the platform, is one of the most concentrated experiences of living Kyoto culture available to a visitor. No tickets, no reservation required — simply arrive at the Maidono area by 10 am on either day. February 3 (the main Setsubun day) is busier. Arrive 30–45 minutes before each dance set for a good viewing position near the pavilion.

events.name: Sacred New Year's Fire Pilgrimage (をけら詣り, Okera-mairi)
events.time: December 31 from approximately 7:30 pm through January 1 at approximately 5 am. The preparatory 鑽火式 (Sankashine-shiki, sacred fire-kindling) is performed on December 28 at 4 am in a closed, non-public rite.
events.origin: The をけら詣り is one of the most ancient and distinctly Kyoto customs associated with any shrine in Japan. Its origins are rooted in the belief that the new year must begin with a new, ritually pure fire — not ordinary flame but divine fire (御神火, goshinbi) kindled through an ancient friction method (火鑽り, hikiri) using cypress wood, without matches, lighters, or any modern implement. On December 28, in the hour of the Tiger (寅の刻, approximately 4 am), a senior priest retreats alone to the main sanctuary in a state of deep purification and uses a fire-drill (火鑽杵, hikiri-kine) and a fire-board (火鑽臼, hikiri-usu) to coax a new sacred flame from friction alone. This flame — the first fire of the new ritual year — is transferred to a lantern in the main hall, where it burns continuously for the entire year until the next December 28, when it is replaced by another newly-kindled fire. The use of をけら (白朮, byakujutsu — Atractylodes japonica, a plant of the chrysanthemum family) gives the ceremony its name: its dried roots, when burned, emit a powerfully pungent medicinal smoke traditionally believed to expel evil spirits and disease. The custom of ordinary people participating — receiving this flame and carrying it home — developed into the popular Okera-mairi tradition celebrated through the Edo period.
events.meaning: The Okera-mairi is a ritual act of profound continuity: the divine fire that has burned throughout the year is extinguished, and a completely new sacred flame takes its place — a cosmic reset that mirrors the human act of releasing the old year and receiving the new. To carry this fire home and use it to cook the first meal of the new year is to bring the living presence of Susanoo's blessing into one's kitchen, one's household, one's body — the god literally enters the family through the medium of the flame. The をけら smoke, carrying the spirit of a medicinal herb, adds the dimension of bodily health: the fire that purifies the soul also heals the flesh. It is one of the most tangible expressions of the connection between shrine and daily life that Shinto has produced.
events.ritual: On December 31, the Ōharae-shiki (大祓式, Great Purification Ceremony) is performed at 3 pm to cleanse the accumulated pollution of the second half of the year. At 7 pm, the Joya-sai (除夜祭, Year-End Festival) is celebrated in the main sanctuary. Immediately after, at approximately 7:30 pm, priests carry the sacred flame from inside the main hall to three をけら灯籠 (Okera lanterns) suspended throughout the shrine grounds. Into these lanterns they place をけら wood — pieces of wood bearing wishes written by worshippers throughout the year — along with fragments of the byakujutsu medicinal root, which begin to smolder and release their characteristic pungent scent into the winter air. Worshippers purchase a 吉兆縄 (kicchō-nawa, auspicious fire-rope, approximately ¥1,000 at stalls within the shrine grounds) — a tightly braided bamboo-fiber cord that smolders slowly without burning quickly. They hold the rope's tip to the lantern flame until it catches, then swing it in slow circles as they walk — the rotation keeps the ember alive without fanning it into open flame. The sight of hundreds of people slowly circling their glowing ropes in the cold dark air of Kyoto's New Year's Eve is one of the most unearthly visual experiences in Japanese festival culture. The lanterns burn from dusk until 5 am on January 1, when the final rite of the season, the Okera-sai (白朮祭), is performed in the main sanctuary. The charred remains of the fire-rope, once extinguished, are kept in the kitchen as a fire-protection (火伏せ) talisman for the year.
events.prayer: Participants pray for mubyō-sokusai (無病息災) — freedom from illness and the safety of the body through the coming year — the singular, unambiguous prayer of this ceremony. By using the sacred fire to cook the first meal of the year (traditionally ozōni, the New Year's rice cake soup), the household physically ingests the blessing, making health not a hope but an act of consumption. The lingering rope talisman in the kitchen extends this prayer materially throughout the year, a daily reminder of the divine fire that started the year.

type.notes: Uniquely Yasaka — this rite exists nowhere else in Japan in this form and is one of the most intimate and participatory sacred experiences available to any visitor to Kyoto. You do not watch the Okera-mairi; you do it. Purchase a kicchō-nawa at the stalls, receive the sacred flame at one of the lanterns (approach one of the attendants if you are uncertain), and join the slow procession of fire-swinging pilgrims through the dark shrine grounds. The experience is most atmospheric between 8 pm and 11 pm, before the midnight New Year's crowds arrive. Practical note: you cannot legally carry a lit rope on public transportation — the rope is designed to be extinguished before you leave the shrine precinct, and carried home as a cold charm. The fire ritual itself takes place entirely within the shrine and no special arrangements are needed to participate.

events.name: Midsummer Great Purification — Passing Through the Reed Ring (大祓式・茅の輪くぐり, Ōharae-shiki / Chinowa Kuguri)
events.time: June 30. Ōharae ceremony at 3 pm. The large chinowa ring is installed in the shrine grounds from mid-June and open to worshippers throughout the day until the end of June.
events.origin: The Chinowa Kuguri — purification by passing through a great ring woven from miscanthus reeds — is grounded in one of the most celebrated myths directly tied to Yasaka Shrine's main deity. Recorded in the Bingo no Kuni Fudoki (備後国風土記, an 8th-century geographical record), the tale tells of Susanoo traveling through the southern seas, tired and hungry, seeking shelter for the night. He came to the home of two brothers: Kyōtan Shorai (巨旦将来), who was wealthy, and Somin Shorai (蘇民将来), who was poor. The rich brother refused to offer shelter; the poor brother, despite having almost nothing, welcomed Susanoo in with food made from millet and a humble bed of straw. Moved by this act of pure-hearted hospitality, Susanoo revealed his divine identity and made a promise: 'In future times, when pestilence spreads, tell people that you are a descendant of Somin Shorai, tie a ring of reeds around your waist, and I will spare you.' This promise is the founding myth of the entire Gion faith — the reason Yasaka Shrine and its 2,300 affiliated shrines exist. Every chinowa ring at Yasaka is Somin Shorai's ring, renewed each year. Every person who passes through it performs the same protective act that Susanoo prescribed sixteen centuries ago.
events.meaning: The Ōharae on June 30 marks the halfway point of the ritual year — the moment when the accumulated spiritual pollution (罪穢れ, tsumi-kegare) of the first six months must be expelled before the second half can begin cleanly. Unlike the Setsubun-sai, which focuses on external demons, the Great Purification addresses the internal contamination of everyday human life: careless words, broken promises, moments of unkindness, contact with death or illness — all the ordinary ways in which people become spiritually heavy over time. The chinowa ring is the physical mechanism of this shedding: to pass through it is to leave contamination behind you on the reed fibers, which are then ritually burned. At Yasaka specifically, this rite is inseparable from the Somin Shorai myth — the same myth that provides the theological foundation of the Gion Matsuri — making the June 30 purification the closing bracket of the spiritual year that the July festival will then open anew.
events.ritual: From mid-June, a large chinowa ring woven from miscanthus reeds (茅の輪, chinowa) is erected within the shrine grounds. Worshippers pass through it following a prescribed path — entering from the front, turning left, re-entering, turning right, re-entering once more, and passing through a final time — a figure-eight pattern said to maximize the purifying effect. Small protective charms inscribed with 'Somin Shorai no Shison Nari' (蘇民将来之子孫也) — the descendant's pledge — are available throughout June. On June 30, the formal Ōharae-shiki ceremony is held by shrine priests at 3 pm: paper cut-out human figures (人形, hitogata) on which worshippers have written their names and breathed their accumulated pollution are gathered and ritually destroyed in water, symbolically washing away the contamination that has been transferred to the paper surrogate. Following the ceremony, the chinowa is removed.
events.prayer: Participants pray for the removal of the first half-year's accumulated tsumi-kegare — spiritual impurity, bad luck, and the invisible weight of everyday transgression — and for a clean, uncontaminated entry into the second half of the year. Those who plan to visit Yasaka during the Gion Matsuri in July have particular reason to undergo this purification first: to arrive at the festival with a cleared spirit is to receive its blessings at full strength.

type.notes: The Chinowa Kuguri is practiced at many shrines across Japan, but at Yasaka it carries a depth of meaning unavailable elsewhere — the ring is physically and mythologically continuous with the Somin Shorai legend that gave birth to the Gion Matsuri. Visiting on or just before June 30 and passing through the ring is therefore not merely a generic purification ritual but a re-enactment of the founding promise that Susanoo made to a poor and generous man, an act that connects you directly to the theological heart of this shrine. The experience is quiet, personal, and takes only a few minutes. For visitors planning a July trip for the Gion Matsuri, arriving in late June to do both the Chinowa Kuguri on June 30 and then the Matsuri in July creates a complete ritual arc of purification followed by celebration.

shrine: Tsushima Shrine (津島神社) — also known as Tennō-sōhonsha (全国天王総本社, Grand Headquarters of all Tennō Shrines)
deities: Takehaya Susanoo no Mikoto (建速須佐之男命) — the storm god, slayer of Yamata no Orochi, and divine protector against pestilence and calamity; worshipped here under his syncretic title Gozu Tennō (牛頭天王, Bull-headed Heavenly King)
Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto (大穴牟遅命) — enshrined as a companion deity; a god of nation-building, medicine, and agricultural abundance

events.name: Owari Tsushima Tennō Festival (尾張津島天王祭, Owari Tsushima Tennō-sai)
events.time: The 4th Saturday of July (Yoimatsuri — Evening Festival) and the following Sunday (Asamatsuri — Morning Festival). Originally performed on the 14th and 15th days of the 6th lunar month. Core public spectacle: Yoimatsuri lantern boats from approximately 9 pm on Saturday; Asamatsuri danjiri-bune procession from approximately 5 am on Sunday.
events.origin: The Tennō Festival is one of Japan's three great river festivals, alongside the Tenjin Festival of Osaka's Tenmangu and the Kangen-sai of Itsukushima Shrine in Hiroshima — and at approximately 600 years of continuous history, it is one of the oldest of the three. Its precise founding date is unclear due to limited early records, but the oldest surviving written record of the festival boats — the Ōsai Ikadaba Kuruma Kiroku — dates from Daiei 2 (1522), confirming the ceremony was already established by the early 16th century. Three theories of origin are documented by Tsushima City authorities. The first concerns the warrior-loyalists of the Southern Court during the Nanbokuchō period: four samurai clans of Tsushima (the Shike-Nana-Myōji, 四家七苗字) who protected Prince Yoshiō, a great-grandchild of Emperor Go-Daigo, lured their Northern Court enemies onto boats under the pretense of pleasure-cruising and defeated them — the festival subsequently commemorated this act of river-borne loyalty. The second is mythological: when Susanoo no Mikoto arrived by boat from the western sea to Ichie Island (present-day Higashihomachi, Aisai City), he watched reed-cutting children playing along the water's shore and was moved to compose children's dances and the melody of the Tsushima Flute; later, when plague struck, the people performed these arts to comfort and pacify the god. The third traces the festival to the ancient capital's Goryōe (御霊会) tradition of floating sacred reeds (神葭流し, Kamiashi-nagashi) down river channels to carry away the plague spirits of summer — a practice that spread from the court city outward to the port towns of the provinces. Tsushima, as a major harbor town connecting Ise and Owari by water, was a natural receiver of this current.
events.meaning: The Tennō Festival is a water-borne expression of the same cosmic logic as the Gion Matsuri of Kyoto — which is its direct theological ancestor. Susanoo, the storm god, brings both the pestilence of summer and its cure; the festival exists to soothe his violent aspect (荒御魂, aramitama) and invite his benevolent aspect (和御魂, nigimitama) to protect the community through the dangerous heat season. The choice of water as the medium is inseparable from Tsushima's identity: this was a port city, its life organized around the movement of ships, and so the divine procession takes place on boats rather than streets. The lanterns of the Yoimatsuri carry a specific cosmological encoding: each of the five boats displays 365 lanterns arranged in a perfect hemisphere (representing the days of the year) around a central pillar bearing 12 lanterns (the months) — or 13 in a leap year, acknowledging the old lunar calendar. The boats are therefore not merely beautiful; each is a complete model of time itself, floating on the dark water, mapping the year onto light.
events.ritual: The festival spans the entire summer season through a sequence of preparatory rites, with the climax on two days in July. In the weeks before, the Kamiashi-nagashi (神葭流し) — the sacred reed-floating purification — is performed, in which priests float bundles of sacred reeds downstream to carry away the plague spirits that accumulate in summer heat. On Saturday evening, from approximately 7 pm, lanterns are lit on the five Makiwara-bune (巻藁船, straw-wound lantern boats) of the five Tsushima villages — Komenoza, Tōge, Ikadaba, Imaichiba, and Shimokamai. By 9 pm the fully illuminated boats glide out from Kurumakawado onto the Tennōkawa pond, their 365 candle-lit lanterns reflected in the still black water, while musicians aboard play the haunting Tsushima Flute (津島笛) — an ancient melodic tradition unique to this festival. As each boat reaches the Otabisho (御旅所, the temporary riverside resting place of the deity's portable shrine), the passengers disembark and bow before Susanoo's presence before returning to their vessels. On Sunday morning, the five boats undergo a complete overnight transformation — lanterns are removed and replaced with two-tiered festival structures bearing Noh-costume figures (置物, okimono) representing characters from classical Noh plays. The lead boat always carries the figure of Takasago (高砂), the Noh play about longevity and conjugal love, while the four remaining boats cycle through different Noh programs each year. The Ichie-guruma (市江車) — the boat from the village of Ichie in present-day Aisai City — leads the procession of six Danjiri-bune (車楽船) decorated festival boats as they make their way along the pond. At the critical moment near Nakanoshima, ten young men (布鉾持, nunohoko-mochi) from the Ichie boat leap into the water in loincloth, swimming across the pond holding their sacred fabric-spears (布鉾) through the current, then run dripping to the shrine to offer them at the main hall. Child ritual attendants (稚児, chigo) from each boat are then ferried ashore at the Otabisho for the Mikoshi Kankyo-sai (神輿還御祭, the return procession of the deity's portable shrine), and the procession moves together back to the main shrine for the closing ceremony and the sacred music performed by the chigo before the deity.
events.prayer: The festival is fundamentally a prayer for ekibyō-yoke (疫病除け) — protection from epidemic disease — and for the safe passage through the dangerous heat of midsummer, which in pre-modern Japan was the season of cholera, typhoid, and dysentery. The lanterns offered to the god represent the entire year laid before him as an act of sustained gratitude and petition. The Kamiashi-nagashi reed ceremony prays specifically for the expulsion of plague spirits from the community. Participants and observers pray for their own and their families' health, for the prosperity of business and livelihood, and for the continuation of the community's special relationship with Susanoo across another year.

type.notes: One of the most visually magnificent festivals in central Japan and profoundly undervisited compared to its cultural stature. The Saturday evening Yoimatsuri is the unmissable event — arrive at the Tennōkawa pond (天王川公園の丸池) by 8 pm for a good viewing position along the bank. The slow emergence of the lantern boats from the dark shore, the reflection of 365 candles in the still water, and the strange, reedy sound of the Tsushima Flute are unlike anything else in Japanese festival culture. The Sunday morning Asamatsuri begins very early (boats typically appear from around 5–6 am) and features the dramatic spectacle of the Ichie young men leaping into the water — arrive by 5 am for the best position. Designated National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (国指定重要無形民俗文化財) and UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage (2016). Tsushima is approximately 30 minutes by train from Nagoya on the Meitetsu Tsushima Line.

events.name: Reed-Torch Opening Gate Festival (開扉祭, Kaihi-sai) — also known as Omito (おみと)
events.time: The 1st day of the 2nd lunar month (旧暦2月1日), falling in late February or March by the solar calendar. In 2026: March 19. The torch procession begins at approximately 7:45 pm; main ceremony from 8 pm.
events.origin: The Kaihi-sai — popularly known as Omito, a contraction of the ancient name Mitohiraki Shinji (御戸開神事, the Opening of the Sacred Gate ceremony) — is the second most important festival in the Tsushima Shrine calendar, with over 700 years of continuous history. Its ancient name was Yuki no Kusai (由貴供祭), a term that parallels the Yuki-no-Ōmiketachi of Ise Jingū, signifying a specially pure and elaborate offering. The festival marks the arrival of spring — the moment when the world reopens after winter's closure — symbolized by the opening of the main sanctuary's inner doors, which are rarely opened to the outside world. The use of giant torches woven from reed (葭, yoshi) is unique in the Owari region and directly reflects Tsushima's geography as a water-plain settlement: this land grows reeds, not pines or cypress, and the sacred fire here is built from the most abundant plant of the wetlands. The ancient belief encoded in the ceremony is that as spring approaches and the world begins to stir again, the boundary between the human world and the realm of evil spirits becomes dangerously permeable — sacred fire (斎火, imi-bi) must be used to burn away the approaching calamity before it can take hold.
events.meaning: The Kaihi-sai is the annual renewal of the covenant between Susanoo no Mikoto and the community of Tsushima. At its ceremonial heart is the Hōhei (奉幣, sacred offering of divine emblems) — the updating and redistribution of the sacred wands that embody Susanoo's divine presence, sent from the main sanctuary to all the subordinate shrines within the Tsushima network. In Tsushima Shrine's theology, the Hōhei is not a mere formality but the most sacred act of the entire ritual year: it is the physical renewal of Susanoo's protective power, the recharging of the divine batteries that keep pestilence, lightning, and crop-eating insects at bay for another year. The towering reed torches — each 10 meters long, 1 meter in diameter — are not simply illumination; they are purifying fires that cleanse the space in which this renewal takes place, driving away the evil that gathers at the threshold of spring.
events.ritual: The two great reed torches are made by hand in the weeks before the festival. Sacred reeds are harvested from the shrine's own designated wetland fields in Aisai City, then tightly wound and bound with straw rope in a labor-intensive process that builds each torch to 10 meters in length and 1 meter in diameter. On the evening of the festival, the two completed torches are moved from in front of the shrine office to the eastern great torii gate. At approximately 7:45 pm, the sacred flame (清火, seibi) is transferred to both torches. From this moment, 25 carriers per torch — 50 members of the Tsushima City Fire Brigade serving in formal dedication — shoulder the enormous burning torches and begin a measured procession: through the eastern precinct, around the arched bridge (反り橋), then in a single surge through the Rōmon gate (the great Momoyama-period gate donated by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1591) into the inner precinct, where both torches are planted upright before the worship hall. At this exact moment, priests advance between the walls of flame into the main sanctuary, where the inner doors are opened and the special ritual food offering (特殊神饌) on tall lacquered stands (高杯, takatsuki) is presented in the innermost chamber. The Hōhei — the annual renewal of sacred offerings to all shrines — is then performed while the twin torches burn beside the hall. The torches are lowered, unbound, and allowed to burn completely. As the reeds smolder down to ash and char, the assembled worshippers compete to seize the burnt remnants — used as chopsticks they are believed to cure toothache and prevent stroke (中風除け); left in the rice paddies they drive away crop-eating insects (田の虫除け); hung in the home they provide protection from lightning (雷除け).
events.prayer: Participants pray for renewal at the passage of winter into spring — for the expulsion of all accumulated calamity (厄除け・災難除け) from the previous year and protection from the dangers that stir as the earth warms. The scramble for the burnt reed remnants is itself a form of prayer — a physical act of seizing the deity's blessing before it dissipates into the air.

type.notes: Highly recommended as a dramatically raw and visually overwhelming experience — far less touristically known than the Tennō Festival but arguably more viscerally powerful. The sight of two 10-meter burning reed columns being shouldered through the old Momoyama gate in complete darkness is one of the most primally impressive ritual moments in Aichi Prefecture. The festival begins at 8 pm and the most dramatic action (torch entry through the gate) takes around 20–30 minutes. Note: the inner ceremony after the torches are placed is conducted by priests inside the closed sanctuary — visitors witness the torch procession and the burning, not the inner rites. Confirmed Tsushima City-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property. Always check the current solar calendar date equivalent for the lunar February 1 before visiting, as it shifts each year.

events.name: Spring Prayer Festival and Ancient Field Rite (祈年祭・春縣祭, Kinensai / Haru-agata-sai)
events.time: February 17, from 10:30 am. One of the three great annual festivals (年中三大祭) of Tsushima Shrine.
events.origin: The Kinensai (祈年祭) is Japan's ancient national rite for the blessing of the spring harvest, observed across the country since at least the Heian period — its history at Tsushima mirrors that described for Ise Jingū. What makes Tsushima's spring festival uniquely its own is the paired rite, the Haru-agata-sai (春縣祭, Spring Field Festival), which was until the Meiji period performed separately on the Horse Day of the 1st lunar month. The word 縣 (agata) means agricultural land — this was Tsushima's indigenous spring prayer, rooted in the shrine's ancient role as protector of the wetland rice fields of the Owari plain. It predates the nationally standardized Kinensai and reflects the peculiar relationship between this region's rice cultivation and its waterlogged geography. The two rites were merged onto the same day in the modern era but both are still performed, preserving the double identity of the spring prayer: one universal, one local. A unique accompanying ceremony, the Ōmita-asobi (大御田遊び, the Great Sacred Field Play), is performed — a ritual enactment of rice cultivation movements and agricultural prayers that is among the most ancient folk religious rites still practiced in the region.
events.meaning: The spring festival at Tsushima carries the same cosmological significance as the Kinensai at Ise — the prayer that opens the sacred cycle of the agricultural year, acknowledging that the harvest to come is not a human achievement but a divine gift requiring prior petition. The Haru-agata-sai adds the specifically local dimension: the flooded reed-fields and rice paddies of the Owari lowlands are understood as the sacred domain of Susanoo, the storm god whose waters and winds determine whether the harvest will succeed or fail. To pray for the spring planting here is to negotiate directly with the deity most capable of either destroying or blessing the crop.
events.ritual: The main ceremony follows the standard national structure: Ōmiketachi (大御饌, the great food offering) presented to the enshrined deities, followed by formal prayers for the year's abundance. What is uniquely Tsushima is the Ōmita-asobi (大御田遊び) — an ancient mimed re-enactment of rice cultivation performed by designated participants, representing the full cycle from planting to harvest in stylized movement and chant. After the main ceremony, the Hōhei (奉幣) offerings are carried in procession to the upper sanctuary (上宮, Imori-sha) and lower sanctuary (下宮, Kashiwa-sha), and special ritual shelves (しもと棚) are loaded with food offerings and hung with ritual carp (掛鮒). In former times, the carp were contributed by the Onigumi (御贄組) — twelve fishing villages along the Hinokawa River that held special fishing rights granted by the Owari domain lord — but as the river was polluted and fishing ceased, this direct supply chain between the wetlands and the shrine was broken. The tradition is preserved in form if not in direct substance.
events.prayer: Participants pray for gokoku hōjō (五穀豊穣) — the abundant ripening of the five grains — and for the prosperity of all productive endeavors (諸業繁栄). At a shrine deeply connected to the protection of life from disease and calamity, the spring prayer is also understood as asking Susanoo to hold back the storms, floods, and pests that threaten the crop through the growing season.

type.notes: The ceremony itself is closed to general visitors. The pilgrimage value for a tourist is in arriving at the shrine on February 17 — the day the ancient prayer for spring is offered — and walking the quiet cedar-lined approach to the main hall in the knowledge that a rite unchanged in structure for over a thousand years is being performed inside. February is cold and still in Tsushima, with few visitors; the shrine retains an intimacy in this season that the summer festival crowds cannot offer. Worth combining with a walk through the old merchant town of Tsushima, whose historic streetscape (津島市の歴史的町並み) preserves the atmosphere of the prosperous port city that Nobunaga, Hideyoshi, and Ieyasu all knew.

events.name: Autumn Harvest Thanksgiving and Ancient Field Rite (新嘗祭・秋縣祭, Niinamesai / Aki-agata-sai)
events.time: November 23, from 10:30 am. One of the three great annual festivals (年中三大祭) of Tsushima Shrine.
events.origin: The Niinamesai is Japan's ancient harvest thanksgiving, observed at shrines nationwide on November 23 — the day the Emperor himself tastes the new grain alongside the gods. At Tsushima, as with the spring festival, the national rite is paired with the shrine's own indigenous counterpart: the Aki-agata-sai (秋縣祭, Autumn Field Festival), which gives thanks for the specific harvest of the Owari wetland fields that the Haru-agata-sai petitioned for in February. Together, the spring and autumn pair form the complete agricultural year as understood by this shrine — a closed circle of petition and gratitude that has framed the rice-farming life of the Owari plain for at least a thousand years.
events.meaning: The Niinamesai at Tsushima closes the cosmological arc that the Kinensai opened. The same fields for whose blessing Susanoo was petitioned in February have now yielded their harvest; the deity must be shown the result, fed the first grain, and thanked before the community may consume it. At a shrine whose primary identity is protection from the forces that destroy human life — epidemic, storm, flood — the autumn thanksgiving carries the additional dimension of gratitude for survival: not merely that the crop grew, but that the people who planted it are still alive to harvest it.
events.ritual: The ceremonial structure mirrors the spring festival exactly: Ōmiketachi food offering, formal prayer, Hōhei to the upper and lower sanctuaries, and offerings laid on the ritual shelves (しもと棚) with hanging carp (掛鮒). The harvested rice stalks from the shrine's own sacred paddies (神饌田) are brought as Kakechikara (懸税) offerings — bundles of cut rice hung before the deity in gratitude, the same tradition observed at Ise Jingū. As at the spring festival, the Ōmita-asobi field-mime is also performed in its autumn form.
events.prayer: Those who participate give thanks for the harvest, for the health of their families through the year, and for the protection Susanoo has extended over their livelihoods and fields. They also pray for safe passage through the coming winter — the season when illness and cold take those weakened by the year's labor — renewing their bond with the god who holds both destruction and protection in the same hands.

type.notes: Again a closed ceremony, valuable for the pilgrim rather than the spectator. November 23 is a national holiday (Labor Thanksgiving Day, 勤労感謝の日), which means the shrine will see more visitors than the February Kinensai — but far fewer than summer. Tsushima in late November is quiet, its ginkgo trees golden along the approaches to the shrine. The pairing of Niinamesai with Aki-agata-sai makes this the most complete expression of the shrine's agricultural identity: one ceremony universal, one ceremony uniquely rooted in the wetland rice culture of this specific corner of Aichi. Visitors who have also attended the summer Tennō Festival will find November 23 to be the spiritual counterweight — the stillness and gratitude of autumn harvest against the fire and alarm of the summer plague ritual.

shrine: Hikawa Shrine (氷川神社) — Musashi Ichinomiya (武蔵一宮), formally Musashi Ichinomiya Hikawa Jinja
deities: Susanoo no Mikoto (須佐之男命) — principal deity; storm god, slayer of the Yamata no Orochi, composer of Japan's first waka poem; enshrined here as guardian of water, earth, and the people of the Musashi plain
Kushiinadahime no Mikoto (稲田姫命, also written 奇稲田姫命) — wife of Susanoo, goddess of rice and the harvest, daughter of Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi
