Ōnamuchi no Mikoto (大己貴命) — son of Susanoo and Kushiinadahime; also known as Ōkuninushi, god of nation-building, medicine, and commerce

events.name: Imperial Grand Festival and Sacred Procession (例祭・神幸祭, Reisai / Jinkōsai)
events.time: August 1 (Reisai, Grand Annual Festival) and August 2 (Jinkōsai, Sacred Procession). Preceded by the Eve Festival (宵宮, Yoimiya) on July 31. Reisai begins at 10 am; Jinkōsai begins at 3 pm.
events.origin: The original summer grand festival of Hikawa Shrine was held on the 14th and 15th days of the 6th lunar month — a timing that aligned it with the great summer purification traditions of ancient Japan, including the Gion Matsuri of Kyoto. When Emperor Meiji moved the capital to Tokyo in 1868, his first act of religious statecraft was to declare Hikawa Shrine the guardian deity of Musashi Province and to elevate it to a Chokusha — a shrine receiving the Emperor's direct ceremonial attention. On October 28 of that year, Emperor Meiji traveled to Ōmiya and personally performed the rites, the first emperor to do so since antiquity. Following the Meiji calendar reform in 1872, the festival date was shifted for practical reasons — farming communities petitioned that the lunar 6th month fell during agricultural peak season — and in 1875 the Reisai was fixed to August 1, where it has remained ever since. The pairing of the Reisai (inner sacred ceremony) with the Jinkōsai the following day (the god's procession through the community) reflects an ancient structure: first the deity is honored in the sanctuary, then the deity blesses the community by traveling through it.
events.meaning: The Reisai is the supreme statement of Hikawa Shrine's role as the paramount guardian of the Kantō region — the 'Great Shrine' whose blessing protects not just its immediate community but the entire arc of civilization that spread outward from the Musashi plain. The arrival of the Imperial envoy (勅使) bearing the Emperor's silk offerings (幣帛, heihaku) in five sacred colors is a cosmological act: the Emperor, as descendant of Amaterasu, presents his reverence to Susanoo, the storm god who made the land habitable. The Azuma-asobi (東遊) court dance performed by the Imperial musicians adds an equally ancient layer: this is the very dance tradition brought to the Kantō region as part of the civilizing of the eastern provinces, now offered back to the regional god as a gift of culture. The Jinkōsai on August 2 enacts Susanoo's protective circuit of his domain — the god traveling through the streets he watches over, blessing every street and household his mikoshi passes.
events.ritual: On July 31 (Yoimiya), the shrine precinct is transformed: inside the Rōmon gate, paper lanterns (雪洞, bonbori) painted by devotees are hung throughout the inner precinct, and outside the gate the approach road blazes with high-posted chōchin lanterns (高張提灯) donated by each neighborhood association. On August 1 from 10 am, the full ceremonial procession of priests in ancient court dress (衣冠束帯, ikkan-sōtai) advances through the outer precinct while the town's floats (山車, dashi) and mikoshi portable shrines are assembled along the sacred bridge approach (神橋前参道), flanking the central path in two rows. The Imperial envoy then advances from the designated Chokusha-kan (勅使館, envoy's rest hall) in formal regalia, accompanied by attendants carrying the Emperor's silk offerings and musicians. Because the ceremony is performed inside the closed Rōmon gate, it cannot be directly observed — but the procession approaching and entering is entirely visible from the outer precinct. The highlight of the ceremony inside is the performance of Azuma-asobi (東遊) — ancient Eastern Japanese court dance — by Imperial musicians. This form of dance, first recorded at Tōdaiji in 861 CE and subsequently adopted at the Kamo shrines and Spring Kasuga, consists of four movements: the Ichiuta (一歌), Niuta (二歌), Suruga-uta (駿河歌), and Motome-ko-uta (求子歌), accompanied by Japanese harp (和琴, wagon), Korean flute (高麗笛, koma-bue), hichiriki double reed, and wooden clappers. The dancers wear blue-brushed ceremonial robes and carry seasonal flowers in their headdresses. On August 2 from 3 pm, the Jinkōsai begins: a procession of over 100 participants including priests, court musicians, kagura performers, ceremonial weapon-bearers, and portable shrine carriers (輿丁, yochō) from the six sacred inner communities (忌垣, igaki) — Ōnari, Higashi-Ōnari, Kami-Ochiai, Shinkai, Dote, Hori-no-uchi — who alone are authorized to carry the god's mikoshi. The deity's spirit is transferred from the main hall to the mikoshi, then carried to the sacred bridge (神橋) over the garden pond, where the water-purification ceremony (橋上祭, Kyōjō-sai) is performed using water drawn from the sacred pool below. The offerings include woven wheat-straw mats (小麦藁莚) provided by the ancient shrine estate community — a tradition maintained even after commercial wheat farming ceased in the area, with old families weaving the mats from wheat grown elsewhere to preserve the ancestral obligation. The mikoshi then circuits the pond and returns to the main hall.
events.prayer: The Reisai prayer, voiced by priests on behalf of the Emperor and the entire nation, seeks kokka antai (国家安泰, national peace and stability), gokoku hōjō (五穀豊穣, abundant harvests), and the flourishing of all within the shrine's protection. The Jinkōsai extends this prayer into every street and household the procession passes through — the god's physical presence in the community is understood as a direct act of blessing and protection distributed to all.

type.notes: The most important occasion to visit Hikawa Shrine. The full three-day sequence of July 31 (lantern-lit atmosphere), August 1 (Imperial envoy procession and floats), and August 2 (mikoshi procession) offers layers of experience across different registers. On August 1, arrive by 9 am for a position near the approach road to watch the procession of priests and the Imperial envoy advance through the outer precinct — this is one of the few occasions in the Kantō region where you can observe an active Chokusha ceremony. On August 2, the Jinkōsai procession departs from the main sanctuary at 3 pm and moves through the outer precinct toward the sacred bridge — the sight of the ancient mikoshi being carried by the six igaki communities while priests and musicians follow is deeply moving and requires no special access. The Azuma-asobi performance inside the closed sanctuary on August 1 cannot be seen, but knowing it is happening deepens the experience of standing outside. This combined festival is the closest Kantō equivalent to the Gion Matsuri in terms of its imperial pedigree and ceremonial depth.

events.name: Flower-Calming Festival (鎮花祭, Chinbana-sai / Hanashizume-sai)
events.time: April 5 (minor ceremony), April 6 (middle ceremony), April 7 (grand ceremony 大祭). All ceremonies begin at 10 am at the main sanctuary and Maidono (舞殿) dance pavilion.
events.origin: The Chinbana-sai is one of the oldest recorded national rites in Japan, enshrined in the Taihō Code (大宝律令, 701 CE) as a compulsory state ceremony. The belief encoded in its name — 'calming the flowers' — is grounded in an ancient understanding of disease transmission: when the cherry blossoms scatter in spring, the epidemic spirits (疫神, ekijin) that have been dormant through winter are released into the air along with the petals, dispersed through the landscape in the same warm winds that carry the falling flowers. The Ryō-no-Gige (令義解, a Heian-period legal commentary) explicitly states that the festival was performed because 'when spring flowers scatter, the plague deities distribute and spread epidemic disease.' The primary observance was mandated at Ōmiwa Shrine and Sai Shrine in Yamato (Nara) — shrines of the deities most associated with pestilence control — but the rite spread to major shrines across the country. At Hikawa Shrine, the Chinbana-sai connects directly to Susanoo's identity as the god who both brings and controls the forces of natural disruption — storm, plague, and the chaos of seasonal change.
events.meaning: The Chinbana-sai is a ceremony of cosmic negotiation at the hinge between winter and summer — the moment when the world is most beautiful and most dangerous simultaneously. The scattering cherry blossoms are not simply aesthetic; they are a signal that the boundary between order and chaos is temporarily porous. To calm the flowers is to ask the divine forces of spring to slow their transformation, to hold the season at its peak of beauty a moment longer, and in doing so to prevent the epidemic spirits from riding the petals outward into the community. The girls who perform the Hanashizume no Mai (花しづめの舞, Flower-Calming Dance) embody this negotiation: their youth and purity, their spring colors, and their gentle movements with cherry branches are an offering of beauty that mirrors and responds to the beauty of the blossoms — meeting the divine with its own reflected image as a form of pacification.
events.ritual: On each of the three days, the sacred rites begin in the main sanctuary at 10 am, where offerings including the special 菱餅 (hishi-mochi, diamond-shaped rice cake) — on the grand day adorned with an actual cherry blossom — are presented, and prayers for mubyō-sokusai (無病息災, freedom from disease) and gokoku hōjō are offered. Simultaneously, at the Maidono open-air dance pavilion, six girls selected from among the shrine's parishioner families perform the Hanashizume no Mai. Four older girls are called 乙女 (otome, maidens) and two younger girls are called 蕾 (tsubomi, buds). All wear the 千早 (chihaya, a white layered ceremonial robe) with cherry-blossom ornaments in their hair and carry branches of cherry in their hands. The dance proceeds in two halves: in the first, set to the ancient court song style of Imayō (今様, 'contemporary style' music of the Heian period), the girls carry cherry branches and incorporate the hand gestures of rice planting — a deliberate fusion of the spring purification rite with the agricultural prayer for the coming growing season. In the second half, set to koto music playing the well-known melody of 'Sakura Sakura,' the girls hold both cherry branches and bells, and the younger 'bud' dancers distribute small bells to gathered observers. The ceremony is fully open to anyone who wishes to watch from the outer precinct.
events.prayer: Participants and observers pray for the expulsion of epidemic diseases that gather at the season's turning, for their own health and the health of their families through the spring and summer ahead, and for the smooth beginning of the agricultural year. The girls' dance specifically prays that the scattered cherry petals carry away illness rather than spread it — that the beauty of spring bless rather than harm.

type.notes: The most accessible and visually beautiful ceremony in the Hikawa calendar, and largely unknown outside the local community — making it an ideal experience for a visitor seeking depth without crowds. The grand ceremony on April 7 is the most complete, combining the main sanctuary rites with the full Hanashizume no Mai performance. The sight of six young girls in white robes and cherry ornaments dancing with blossoming branches in the open-air pavilion, surrounded by the ancient trees of the shrine's 30,000-tsubo precinct which are themselves in late cherry bloom, is genuinely extraordinary. No tickets, no reservations — simply arrive at the Maidono by 9:45 am on April 7. The 2-kilometer keyaki (zelkova) lined approach from the first torii near Saitama-Shintoshin station is in full spring greenery at this time, making the walk itself part of the experience.

events.name: Great Sacred Fire and Year-End Market Festival (大湯祭, Daitō-sai)
events.time: Pre-festival (前斎): November 30 through December 9, nightly bonfires at 7:30 pm. Grand Festival (本祭): December 10, ceremony begins at 8 am. Closing rite (後斎): December 11. The Tōka-machi (十日市) market fills the shrine precinct and approach on December 10.
events.origin: The Daitō-sai is the most distinctive and historically deepest of all Hikawa Shrine's special rites, and the one the shrine itself identifies as its most celebrated (最も著名な) special ceremony. Its recorded history begins in 1385 (Shitoku 2), when the Musashishū Adachi Ōmiya Hikawa Taimeijin Engi-no-Sho (武蔵州足立大宮氷川太明神縁起之書) documents a fire-walking ceremony (火剣祭礼, kakensakurei) performed on December 10 — a rite in which burning brushwood was made into a fire-bed and participants walked through or over it. By the Enpo era (1673–1681), the ceremony was already referred to as 'Daitō-sai' in shrine records, and in 1676 the fire-walking element was transformed by the petition of shrine priest Hikawa Naiki (氷川内記) into its current form — a purification ceremony centered on ritual fire rather than fire-walking. The name 'Daitō' (大湯, 'Great Hot Water') derives from the ancient practice of boiling a great cauldron of water and using the steam and hot water to cleanse the ritual space. The festival originated as the ceremony of the Hio-oji-sha (簸王子宮), one of the three co-equal sanctuaries of old Hikawa — the shrine dedicated to Ōnamuchi no Mikoto (大己貴命), the divine physician and commerce god whose blessing is at the heart of the December market.
events.meaning: The Daitō-sai occupies the end of the ritual year as its most intensive period of purification — a 12-day act of progressive sacred cleansing that burns away the accumulated defilements of the year before the new year can begin cleanly. The nightly bonfires of the pre-festival period are not incidental atmosphere; they are continuations of the ancient fire-purification tradition, and the shrine explicitly teaches that standing beside them confers the blessing of mubyō-sokusai (無病息災) and hibuse (火防, fire-protection). The 100 feast offerings (百味膳, hyaku-aji-zen) of the grand ceremony — an enormous assembly of land and sea foods, representing the full abundance of the natural world — present the deity with the richest possible offering at the year's end, a statement of gratitude for everything the year contained. The issuance of the special Ebisu and Daikoku god-cards on this day alone — available at no other time of year — makes December 10 a once-annual dispensing of commercial and domestic blessing that cannot be replaced.
events.ritual: From November 30, the officiating priests enter a 12-day period of purification seclusion (潔斎, kessai) within the shrine precinct, withdrawing progressively from ordinary life to prepare themselves for the grand ceremony. Each evening from November 30 through December 9, at 7:30 pm, bonfires (篝火, kagari-bi) are lit in the inner precinct as the residual expression of the original fire ceremony — their flames visible above the walls, their smoke carrying away defilements. On December 10 from 8 am, the grand ceremony begins with an extraordinary offering assembly: 21 tall lacquered stands (尺三方, shaku-sanbō, larger than standard) are prepared with offerings for each of the three main deities — cooked rice, sacred sake, the special 百味膳 (one meal representing the full spectrum of sea, mountain, river, and field foods in eight varieties each), diamond rice cakes (菱餅), shrimp, mountain yam, and a whole carp on a skewer (串付の大鮒). After the inner ceremony, the shrine's subsidiary shrines each receive their own offerings in a circuit. Simultaneously, the spectacular Tōka-machi (十日市, 'Tenth-Day Market') fills the precinct and the 2-kilometer approach with approximately 350 stalls selling traditional kumade (福熊手, lucky rakes), god-shelves (宮型神棚), and other New Year's goods. The energetic sound of hand-clapping and the rhythmic chant of merchants'締め (shime-daiko) trade-closing ceremonies echo throughout the grounds. The only items available exclusively on this day are the Ebisu-Daikoku fukujin-fuda (恵毘須・大国の御影, sacred printed icons of the prosperity gods) and the matching lucky items — charms that connect the blessing of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto (enshrined at the sub-sanctuary Amatsu-jinja as the god of medicine and commerce) directly to the devotee's household. On December 11, the closing ceremony includes the ancient Kyōzen-shiki (饗膳式) — a ceremonial feast in which priests and envoys gather in the former Imperial envoy's hall and three times intone 'Ikuhisa, Ikuhisa, Ikuhisa' (幾久、幾久、幾久 — 'for many long years') as a blessing for the shrine's continued prosperity, then receive the sacred sake.
events.prayer: Participants at the Daitō-sai pray for year-end purification — the shedding of all spiritual pollution accumulated over the 12 months — and for the commercial and domestic prosperity that Ebisu and Daikoku embody. Those who purchase the exclusive god-cards pray for shōbai hanjō (商売繁盛, flourishing business) and kanai anzen (家内安全, household safety) for the coming year. The bonfires are visited for their own protective power, with the specific belief that standing in their warmth protects against illness and household fires throughout the winter.

type.notes: The Daitō-sai is one of the great year-end market festivals in the Tokyo metropolitan area and the most festive and publicly engaging event in the Hikawa calendar. December 10 is the unmissable day: arrive in the morning to purchase the exclusive Ebisu-Daikoku god-cards before they sell out (the queues at the sub-shrine begin early), then move through the 350-stall market along the approach. The inner ceremony itself begins at 8 am and is not publicly viewable, but the outer precinct and market are fully open all day. The nightly bonfires of the pre-festival period (November 30 – December 9, at 7:30 pm) offer a quieter and more spiritually contemplative experience — standing in a cold December night beside the bonfire within the historic precinct is one of the most atmospheric year-end experiences in Saitama. The 12-day arc from November 30 through December 11 allows visitors to choose their level of engagement, from a brief evening bonfire visit to the full market-day experience on the 10th.

events.name: Midsummer Great Purification — Reed Ring Passing (大祓式・茅の輪くぐり, Ōharae-shiki / Chinowa-kuguri)
events.time: June 30, ceremony at 3 pm. The chinowa reed ring is installed on the sacred bridge (神橋) from mid-June and available for visitors to pass through until the end of June.
events.origin: The Ōharae — Great Purification — is one of the oldest state rites in Japan, formalized in the Taihō Code and observed at shrines across the country on June 30 and December 31. At Hikawa Shrine, the June 30 ceremony carries particular depth because of the shrine's intimate connection to Susanoo — the deity who is both the source of calamity and its cure, the storm god who can send plague and the same god who promised Somin Shorai protection through the reed ring. The setting of the chinowa on the sacred bridge over the shrine's sacred pool adds a uniquely Hikawa dimension: this shrine was founded beside a miraculous spring (the 蛇の池, Ja-no-ike, now accessible to visitors as the sacred source pool), and the purification here takes place over water — the element most associated with Susanoo's power and the element that gives the shrine its name.
events.meaning: The June 30 Ōharae marks the halfway point of the ritual year and carries the same meaning it does at Yasaka Shrine: the accumulated spiritual pollution (罪穢れ, tsumi-kegare) of the first six months must be expelled before the second half can begin cleanly. At Hikawa, the direct mythological connection to Susanoo gives this an additional resonance: the same god who asks his worshippers to pass through the reed ring for protection at Yasaka's Gion Matsuri is enshrined here as the primary deity. The Ōharae at Hikawa is therefore a homecoming — purifying oneself before the god who established the practice.
events.ritual: From mid-June, a great chinowa ring woven from miscanthus reeds (茅の輪) is installed on the sacred bridge (神橋) over the shrine's garden pond. Worshippers pass through it in the prescribed figure-eight pattern — forward, left turn, forward, right turn, forward — which is understood to maximize the purification effect. Paper human-form cutouts (人形, hitogata) on which visitors write their names and breathe their accumulated pollution are distributed; these are then ritually burned or floated in water during the ceremony. At 3 pm on June 30, priests perform the formal Ōharae ceremony with the recitation of the Great Purification Prayer (大祓詞, Ōharae-no-kotoba), one of the most ancient and beautiful sacred texts in the Shinto canon. The chinowa ring talisman (茅の輪守) is available for purchase at 1,000 yen and is designed to be hung in the home for protection through the coming six months.
events.prayer: Participants pray for the cleansing of all accumulated spiritual impurity from the first half of the year, for a clean entry into the summer and autumn seasons, and for health and safety through the six months that follow. For those planning to celebrate Obon (mid-August) and the new harvest season, this purification is understood as the prerequisite — arriving at those celebrations with a cleared spirit.

type.notes: The Ōharae and chinowa-kuguri at Hikawa Shrine is a deeply participatory experience — unlike most shrine ceremonies here which are observed from outside, this one requires your physical passage through the ring. The sacred bridge setting over the garden pond, with the ancient keyaki trees and the shrine's famously still atmosphere in late June, makes this one of the most meditative visits possible at the shrine. The ceremony itself takes only a few minutes of your time, but the entire precinct in late June — with its iris-planted garden, the Ja-no-ike sacred spring, and the long, tunnel-like canopy of the 2-kilometer approach — is worth a longer exploration. Early morning visits (before 9 am) in this season are especially peaceful, as the approach's 600-tree canopy creates an extraordinary green light in the early summer sun.

location: Sadacho Susa, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture
deities: Susanoo no Mikoto (須佐之男命 / 須佐能袁命) — the storm god, slayer of the Yamata no Orochi, composer of Japan's first waka poem; enshrined here not as a symbol but as his literal living spirit (御魂, mitama)
Inata-hime no Mikoto (稲田比売命) — wife of Susanoo, the princess saved from the Orochi, goddess of the rice harvest
Ashinazuchi no Mikoto (足摩槌命) — father of Inata-hime, elder god of the land
Tenazuchi no Mikoto (手摩槌命) — mother of Inata-hime, ancestral deity of the Susa clan who serve as hereditary priests of this shrine

events.name: Grand Annual Festival and Brother-Sister Divine Visitation (例大祭・朝覲祭, Reisai / Chōkin-sai)
events.time: April 18 (Reisai — Grand Annual Festival, with Chōkin-sai procession afterward) and April 19 (Koden-sai — Ancient Transmission Festival, with Ryōō no Mai dance and Momote Shinji archery ceremony in the afternoon)
events.origin: The Reisai on April 18 is the supreme annual rite of Susa Shrine, honoring the enshrined spirit of Susanoo with the highest level of ceremony the shrine performs. What makes Susa's Grand Festival uniquely resonant in all of Japanese mythology is what happens immediately after the inner rites: the Chōkin-sai (朝覲祭). In ancient Chinese court tradition, 'chōkin' (朝覲) referred to a vassal's formal morning audience with the Emperor — a structured act of reporting, presenting oneself, and receiving blessing from a superior. At Susa Shrine, the Chōkin-sai re-enacts a scene of mythological reunion: the spirit of Susanoo, settled in this valley, makes a ritual visitation to the small subsidiary shrine of Amaterasu-sha (天照社) on the opposite side of the main precinct — a meeting of the divine storm brother with the enshrined presence of his elder sister, the sun goddess, who drove him from the Plain of Heaven. This annual reconciliation, enacted in formal procession, is a ceremony without parallel at any other shrine.
events.meaning: The Reisai affirms that this valley in Izumo is not merely a place where Susanoo was worshipped, but the place where he chose to rest — the sacred land he named after himself as an act of permanent commitment. The Chōkin-sai adds a layer of profound mythological complexity: Susanoo, who was banished from heaven by Amaterasu after a catastrophic quarrel, visits his sister's shrine in an annual gesture that can be read as reconciliation, reporting, or simply the maintenance of a divine sibling bond across their cosmic separation. This is one of very few ritual reenactments in Japanese Shinto that physically enacts a relationship between two specific deities across two specific shrines within a single precinct — a living performance of myth.
events.ritual: On April 18, the formal Reisai ceremony is performed inside the main sanctuary — offerings are presented, prayers recited, and the full Shinto liturgy honored. Immediately following, the Chōkin-sai procession forms: priests in formal robes carry the symbolic presence of Susanoo in a solemn procession across the precinct to the Amaterasu-sha, where a formal offering ceremony (神饌奉供) is performed. This procession is visible to worshippers in the outer precinct. On April 19, the Koden-sai (古伝祭, Festival of Ancient Transmission) begins with inner rites, after which two remarkable ceremonies are performed openly. First, the Ryōō no Mai (陵王舞, Dragon King Dance) is performed — a ceremonial dance recorded by the shrine as representing the acts of 'Shuri-Kosei, Koden-Hashyu' (修理固成・耕田播種), the twin divine acts of repairing and completing the land and of plowing and sowing the fields. The shrine notes that it was anciently performed by a hereditary sacred functionary called the Ryūō-ke (竜王家, Dragon King family) — a lineage of yin-yang specialists (陰陽師系) whose ritual tradition was absorbed into the shrine's ceremonial life and transformed over centuries into its current form. Second, the Momote Shinji (百手神事, Hundred-Hand Sacred Archery) is performed in the afternoon — a series of ritual bow shots aimed at targets in the designated sacred area, each shot understood as driving away malevolent forces and calling down the blessing of the harvest.
events.prayer: The Reisai and Koden-sai together pray for the protection of the community, the abundance of the harvest, and the expulsion of evil (悪魔退散, akuma-taisan). The Chōkin-sai carries the specific spiritual charge of renewing the relationship between Susanoo and the land he chose — a prayer that the god remains committed to his chosen home and that his protective power continues to flow from this valley into the world.

type.notes: The most theologically profound festival at Susa Shrine, and the one that most directly enacts the unique mythological status of this place. The Chōkin-sai procession on April 18 is visible from the outer precinct and is the closest a visitor can come to witnessing the divine sibling reunion of Susanoo and Amaterasu enacted in ritual form. The Ryōō no Mai and Momote Shinji on April 19 are performed in the open precinct and require no special access. The village of Susa is remote — approximately 40 minutes by car from Izumo City — but April brings the Chugoku mountains into spring bloom, and the journey through the narrow valley of the Susa River to this small precinct in its mountain basin is itself a powerful preparation for the experience.

events.name: Sacred Flower Opening Ritual and Nenbutsu Dance (切明神事・念仏踊り, Kiriake Shinji / Nenbutsu Odori)
events.time: August 15, afternoon. Inner Kiriake Shinji ceremony from 11 am; Nenbutsu Odori dance performance from 3 pm.
events.origin: The Kiriake Shinji (切明神事, literally 'the Opening of the Cut') is Susa Shrine's summer festival, centered on a rite of welcoming the divine into a flower-adorned sacred space and praying for the autumn harvest. The word 'kiriake' refers to the opening and clearing of a ritual space by cutting and arranging the sacred ceremonial flowers (神事花, jinji-bana) — enormous artificial flower constructions, each approximately 3 meters tall, built on bamboo frames decorated with cherry blossoms in artificial bloom. The Nenbutsu Odori (念仏踊り, Nenbutsu Dance) that accompanies it is one of the most historically layered pieces of sacred folk performance in Izumo. Its origins, as recorded by the Izumo Ancient History Museum and the shrine's own preservation society, are traced to a prayer for rain during a great drought (大旱魃, daikanbatsu), when the community first danced to petition the gods for water. The earliest confirmed material record is a ritual bell (鉦) inscribed in the 8th year of Hōei (宝永八年, 1711), preserved by the Susa Ōmiya Nenbutsu Odori Preservation Society. In the Edo period, seven villages of the Susa district each contributed their own dancers and their own jinji-bana flower tower; today only the Susa district's Tsukawaki community continues the tradition. Academic analysis identifies the dance as a hybrid: a rice-planting ritual dance (田楽, dengaku) of ancient agricultural origin, into which itinerant Buddhist chanting priests (念仏聖, nenbutsu-hijiri) of the medieval period wove their distinctive elements — giving it its haunting chant of 'Nā-Mami-Dō' (ナーマミドー, a folk corruption of Namu Amida Butsu) and its meditative, circular, repetitive movement structure. It is designated a Shimane Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property.
events.meaning: The Kiriake Shinji embodies the ancient agricultural theology of Izumo: that the harvest requires not only human labor but the active invitation of divine presence into the fields. The sacred flower towers (jinji-bana) are not decorative — they are the physical structure that draws the god's attention and spirit down from the realm above into the mortal world below, much as the yorishiro floats of the Gion Matsuri attract and contain divine power. The Nenbutsu Odori's circular formation around the base of the flower tower is a sustained act of summoning — the chant, the flute, the unbroken circle of dancers in flowing robes, barefoot on the earth — creating an unceasing, meditative petition that the deity will bless the coming autumn. The dance's hybrid nature — Shinto ritual structure fused with Buddhist chanting — is a rare and honest artifact of Japan's centuries of shinbutsu shūgō (神仏習合), the syncretic blending of the two faiths, preserved intact in this mountain valley long after that syncretic tradition was officially suppressed in the Meiji period.
events.ritual: On the morning of August 15, the inner Kiriake Shinji ceremony is performed at the main sanctuary: priests formally open the sacred space, offer food and prayers to Susanoo, and invoke his blessing upon the approaching harvest season. Two great jinji-bana flower towers — each built by one of the two rotation districts (上地区 and 下地区) of the Susa community — are erected in the precinct before the performance. By 3 pm, the Nenbutsu Odori begins. The dancers from the Tsukawaki preservation society, wearing flowing kimono (着流し) and elaborate flower hats (花笠), form a circle around the base of the great flower towers. Without shoes, their bare feet touching the earth of the precinct, they begin the characteristic slow, circling movement — stepping, turning, stepping — while the lead chanter intones 'Nā-Mami-Dō' in a hypnotic continuous call and the ensemble responds. Flute and bells accompany throughout. The tempo is deliberately slow and unvarying, creating an almost trance-like atmosphere in the evening air. After the ceremony, the jinji-bana flower towers are taken apart and distributed among participants — a branch of the sacred artificial cherry blossom carried home and placed in the rice paddies or fields is believed to ward off the insects and pests (田の虫除け) that can destroy the autumn harvest.
events.prayer: Participants and community members pray specifically for hōsaku (豊作, abundant harvest) in the approaching autumn — for rain in its proper season, for freedom from the insects and blight that can devastate the mountain rice fields of Izumo, and for the community's survival through the year. The rain-prayer origin of the dance is still present in its structure: the circular movement with bare feet is understood as an act of calling water — both literally (rainfall) and spiritually (divine blessing that flows like water).

type.notes: One of the most rare and atmospherically powerful folk ritual performances in Shimane Prefecture, and one that almost no tourist outside the Izumo region encounters. The Nenbutsu Odori begins at 3 pm and lasts approximately one hour. The combination of the remote mountain-basin setting, the enormous artificial cherry-blossom towers, the barefoot dancers chanting in a slow circle, and the flute music in the late summer afternoon makes this one of the most genuinely otherworldly ritual experiences in western Japan. Arrive by 2 pm to observe the setup of the jinji-bana before the performance begins. August 15 is Obon — the road through the valley will have more traffic than usual as local families return for the ancestral festival, adding a layer of communal atmosphere to the shrine visit. The Susanoo-kan (スサノオ館) cultural facility adjacent to the shrine contains a permanent display featuring a jinji-bana tower and documentation of the Nenbutsu Odori if you wish to understand the context before witnessing the performance. Shimane Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property.

events.name: Shrine Rebuilding — Reiwa Grand Transfer (令和の御遷宮, Reiwa no Gosen-gū)
events.time: The temporary transfer (仮殿遷座祭) was performed April 19, 2025. The full rebuilding and final return ceremony (本殿遷座祭) is scheduled to conclude in 2026. The 2026 Grand Festival (例大祭, April 18) will be celebrated as a special Sengu-commemoration event with dedicated ceremonial seals and rites.
events.origin: The current main hall of Susa Shrine dates to 1554 (天文23年), when it was newly constructed by the Izumo warlord Amago Haruhisa (尼子晴久). In the 470 years since its construction, the shrine has followed the tradition of major repairs approximately every 30 years, with each generation of the Susa priestly family taking responsibility for the preservation of this singular sacred structure. The Reiwa Sengu (令和の御遷宮) — the current rebuilding and renovation — was triggered by the accumulated deterioration of the main hall structure over decades. Because this shrine enshrines Susanoo's actual living mitama, the Sengu at Susa carries a weight that differs from the periodic rebuildings at other shrines: it is not merely a structural renovation but a direct engagement with the god's own dwelling, requiring the careful, prayerful temporary relocation of his living spirit (御魂) to a provisional sanctuary while his permanent home is restored.
events.meaning: Unlike the Shikinen Sengū at Ise Jingū — which is scheduled on a fixed 20-year cycle as a philosophical statement about perpetual renewal — Susa Shrine's Sengu is driven by the actual condition of the ancient structure. This makes it a more intimate and urgent event: the community is not performing a cosmic philosophical ritual but tending to the home of a god who is genuinely, specifically, irreplaceably present in this place. The Sengu at Susa is also an act of communal identity — the Susa valley's entire existence as a named, inhabited, culturally coherent place derives from Susanoo's act of naming it. To maintain the shrine is to maintain the foundational act of naming itself.
events.ritual: On April 19, 2025 — the day of the annual Koden-sai, chosen for its ancient sacred significance — the Honden Kariden Senza-sai (本殿仮殿遷座祭, Main Hall Temporary Transfer Ceremony) was performed: Susanoo's mitama was formally, carefully, and with the deepest reverence carried from the main hall to a temporary provisional sanctuary within the precinct. During the rebuilding period, all regular shrine ceremonies continue at the temporary sanctuary. When the restored main hall is complete, the Honden Senza-sai (本殿遷座祭, Main Hall Return Ceremony) will be performed as the concluding moment of the Sengu — the god returning to his restored home. A special commemorative御朱印 (goshuin, sacred stamp) marking the Sengu period is available at the shrine office during the rebuilding.
events.prayer: Those who visit during the Sengu period pray for the successful completion of the restoration, for the continued protection of this irreplaceable sacred space, and for the blessings of Susanoo upon the craftspeople and community members who are sustaining his dwelling. A visit during the Sengu period carries the specific meaning of witnessing Susa Shrine in a state of active, living care — the community demonstrating, in the most concrete possible way, that Susanoo's choice to settle in this valley is honored and reciprocated across the generations.

type.notes: The Sengu at Susa Shrine is a once-in-a-generation event at one of Japan's most mythologically singular sacred places. Visiting during the active rebuilding period — with the god's spirit temporarily housed in the provisional sanctuary, the main hall in a state of sacred repair, and the community gathered in heightened attention around their most important responsibility — is an experience of living Shinto that no guidebook can manufacture. The 2026 Grand Festival (April 18) with its special Sengu commemorative rites is the optimal time to visit during this window. The shrine is small, deeply rural, and set in a valley of extraordinary quiet — the approach through mountain cedar forest, the smell of the Susa River, the 1,300-year-old camphor tree behind the sanctuary — make this one of the most genuinely moving pilgrimage sites in all of Shimane Prefecture regardless of the Sengu. Check the official shrine website (susa-jinja.jp) or the dedicated Sengu site (susajinja-sengu.com) for the current schedule of return ceremonies.

events.name: Bean-Scattering Purification and Sacred Dance Festival (節分祭, Setsubun-sai)
events.time: February 3. Sacred kagura (神楽) performed in the morning; chinowa (茅の輪) reed ring distributed to worshippers; bean-scattering (豆撒き) ceremony performed.
events.origin: The Setsubun at Susa Shrine is the annual turning-of-the-seasons rite observed across Japan, but it takes on particular meaning here because of Susanoo's identity as the god who controls the wild, violent forces that calamity and illness ride — the same god whose myth of expelling the Yamata no Orochi is, at its deepest level, a story about the defeat of the destructive principle by a combination of divine power and human ingenuity. To scatter beans at Setsubun — shouting 'Oni wa soto, fuku wa uchi!' (鬼は外、福は内 — Demons out, good fortune in!) — is to participate, symbolically but genuinely, in the same cosmic act that Susanoo performed against the great serpent. Uniquely at Susa Shrine, the Setsubun celebration also includes the distribution of actual chinowa reed rings (茅の輪) to individual worshippers — a custom that elsewhere is typically limited to the summer Ōharae — with the specific protective power of the 'Somin Shorai no Shison Nari' tradition invoked for household protection through the year.
events.meaning: The Setsubun at a Susanoo shrine carries its own cosmological specificity: Susanoo is not merely a generic protective deity asked to ward off vague misfortune. He is the god who specifically knows what it means to face a monster — to stand between a vulnerable person (Inata-hime) and a vast, devouring force (the Orochi) and to defeat it through both courage and cunning. To pray at his shrine on the night when the boundary between the old year and the new is thinnest, when the spirits of illness and calamity are most active, is to invoke the specific divine template of that confrontation. The kagura performance added to Susa Shrine's Setsubun — sacred mask-dance that tells stories from the Izumo mythological tradition — amplifies this: the divine stories are performed as an offering and as a communal remembrance of who this god is.
events.ritual: On February 3, sacred kagura (神楽) — Izumo-tradition mask-dance performance of mythological stories — is offered at the shrine. The main Setsubun ceremony is performed, after which beans are scattered by shrine priests and invited community members from the worship hall into the assembled worshippers. The specific custom of Susa Shrine — the distribution of personal chinowa reed rings to individual visitors — is performed: worshippers receive a small woven ring made from miscanthus reed, to be carried home and hung as a protective charm through the coming year. This combination of kagura, bean-scattering, and chinowa distribution makes Susa's Setsubun more ritually layered than the typical single-ceremony Setsubun at most shrines.
events.prayer: Participants pray for yakuyoke (厄除け, expulsion of misfortune) and kenkou (健康, health) for the year ahead, and ask Susanoo specifically — as the god who has personally defeated the greatest monster in Japanese mythology — to stand between their households and the forces of illness, disaster, and ill fortune that circulate at the season's turning. The chinowa received on this day is understood as a personal extension of that protection, carried home as a tangible link to the god's power.

type.notes: The Setsubun at Susa Shrine is the most intimate and personally accessible of the shrine's festivals — quiet, community-scaled, and deeply rooted in the specific identity of this place. February in the Izumo mountain interior is cold and still, the cedar forests white with frost in the morning; the drive through the Susa River valley to the shrine is a journey through winter Izumo at its most austere. The kagura performance, the bean-scattering, and the receipt of a chinowa from the hands of a Susa Shrine priest — the hereditary descendants of Tenazuchi, the father-in-law of Susanoo himself — makes this a deeply personal act of connection to one of the oldest sacred lineages in Japanese religious history. For visitors who cannot be in Shimane for the April or August festivals, February 3 offers the most reflective and uncrowded access to the shrine's essential character.

shrine: Izumo Taisha (出雲大社) — formally Izumo Ōyashiro (いづもおおやしろ)
deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto (大国主大神) — the Great Lord of the Land; divine architect of the visible world, master of the invisible world of fate, destinies, and bonds; enshrined here after the Kunitsukami (国譲り, Transfer of the Land) as sovereign of all that lies beyond human perception
Kotoshironushi no Mikoto (事代主命) — son of Ōkuninushi, enshrined in the West Shrine (西十九社)
Takeminakata no Mikoto (武御名方命) — son of Ōkuninushi, enshrined in the East Shrine
Kisagaihime no Mikoto (蛤貝比売命) and Umugihime no Mikoto (蚶貝比売命) — deities who healed Ōkuninushi after his death by the jealous brothers

events.name: The Month of the Divine Assembly — God-Welcoming, Kamiari Festival, Bond-Tying Grand Ceremony, and Divine Departure (神迎神事・神在祭・縁結大祭・神等去出祭, Kamimukae Shinji / Kamiari-sai / Enmusubi Taisai / Karasade-sai)
events.time: The 10th day of the 10th lunar month (神迎神事, evening) through the 17th day of the 10th lunar month (神等去出祭, afternoon) — a full week in the lunar calendar. Because this follows the old lunar calendar, the solar calendar dates shift each year, always falling in November or early December. In 2026: Divine Welcoming on November 18 (evening); Kamiari-sai November 19–25; Enmusubi Taisai November 23 and 25; Karasade-sai November 25 (afternoon). The entire 神在月 (Month of the Gods) spans one full lunar month — approximately November 9 to December 8 in 2026.
events.origin: The theological foundation of the Kamiari-sai rests on the most cosmologically significant event in Japanese mythology: the Kunitsukami (国譲り). According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto had built and governed the entire world of living things — creating agricultural civilization, developing medicine, establishing the bonds of human society. When the heavenly deities, acting on Amaterasu's authority, came to ask him to cede governance of the visible world to Amaterasu's descendants, he agreed — but only on the condition that a magnificent palace be built for him and that he be venerated as the master of all that lies beyond human sight. The palace built in fulfillment of that promise is Izumo Taisha itself. The Kunitsukami established that Ōkuninushi would henceforth govern the Kamigoto — the invisible world of musubi (むすび, the binding force of all connections), destiny, fate, and the unseen mechanisms that shape human life. It is because Ōkuninushi governs all bonds and connections from Izumo that the gods of the entire nation — who oversee every human life in every locality — must come to him in assembly once a year, bringing the matters of human fate that need to be decided for the coming year. The result is the 神在月: the month when all the deities of Japan gather at Izumo, leaving their home shrines temporarily empty — making the 10th lunar month the Kannazuki (神無月, Month Without Gods) everywhere else in Japan, but the Kamiari-zuki (神在月, Month With Gods) in Izumo alone.
events.meaning: The 神在祭 and its surrounding events represent the single most cosmologically significant annual ritual event in Japanese religious life. The Kami-hakari (神議り, Divine Deliberation) conducted by the assembled deities at the Upper Shrine (上宮, Kaminomiya) 950 meters west of the main precinct determines the bonds and connections of every human life for the coming year — not only romantic bonds (良縁, ryōen) but every form of musubi: the connection between a person and their life partner, their friends, their work, their opportunities, their health, and their destiny. The word 'en-musubi' (縁結び) — often translated as 'matchmaking' — carries the far larger meaning of the binding force that holds all things in relationship with one another. Ōkuninushi presides over this assembly as the master of musubi because it is his divine nature: from the beginning, he was the god who built civilization by forming connections between things — between different lands, between humans and the earth, between the worlds of medicine and healing and the bodies of the sick. During the seven days of the Kami-hakari, the land of Izumo falls under a special protocol of restraint (御忌祭, Oimisai): local people traditionally refrain from music, celebrations, and construction, keeping a respectful silence while the gods conduct their business. The unique Izumo custom of 'shinobi-de' (忍び手, silent claps) replaces the normal four claps during this period — worshippers clap without sound, so as not to disturb the divine deliberations.
events.ritual: The sequence begins at Inasa-no-hama (稲佐の浜), the beach one kilometer west of the shrine, on the evening of the lunar 10th: at 7 pm, a sacred fire (御神火) is lit on the beach, and a sacred rope boundary (注連縄) is drawn around the ritual space. Two Himorogi (神籬, divine seat posts) are erected facing the sea, and beside them the Ryūja-gami (龍蛇神, the Dragon-Serpent deity) — a sea-serpent that serves as the divine herald who precedes the assembled gods — is positioned facing the ocean. Priests conduct the Kamimukae Shinji (神迎神事, God-Welcoming Ceremony) on the beach, approximately 20–30 minutes. When the rite concludes, the Himorogi are wrapped in silk screens (絹垣), the Ryūja-gami takes its position at the head, and a procession forms: the gods, symbolically carried in their wrapped sacred seats, advance from the beach along the road toward the shrine while attendants carry tall paper lanterns and musicians play, with worshippers joining the procession as companions (供奉, gubō). Since 2024, public attendance at the beach ceremony and the procession to the shrine has been restored — participants receive a white paper wand (白御幣) at the beach entrance to mark their role as escort. At the shrine, the Kamimukae-sai (神迎祭) is conducted in the worship hall, welcoming the assembled gods into the precinct. The gods then take up residence in the 十九社 (Jūku-sha, Nineteen Shrines) — the long, low corridor-shrines on the east and west sides of the main precinct, whose doors remain closed all year except during this week, when they stand open as the divine dormitories. Each day of the seven-day period, ceremonies are held at the 上宮 (the Upper Shrine, where the divine deliberations occur) and at the main precinct. The 縁結大祭 (Enmusubi Taisai, Bond-Tying Grand Ceremony) is held on the 15th and 17th days of the lunar month, at 10 am before the main sanctuary: this is a formal prayer ceremony at which registered participants — who must apply in advance by postcard approximately one month before the festival — gather in formal dress before the main hall, receive a 幸縁むすび祈念絵馬 (Kōen-musubi prayer votive), and participate in the ritual that directly petitions the assembled gods and Ōkuninushi for the establishment of good bonds in their lives. The ceremony closes at the 神等去出祭 (Karasade-sai, Divine Departure Ceremony): the gods are assembled from their lodging shrines into the worship hall, an offering of mochi is made, the Kokuzo (出雲国造, the hereditary Grand Priest) intones the prayers of farewell, then a priest strikes the great gate (楼門) three times while calling 'O-hatchi!' (お発ち, Departure!) — and the assembled gods of Japan return to their home shrines to implement the decisions made in Izumo. A Second Karasade-sai is held approximately nine days later at the Mankusen Shrine (万九千神社) in Higashi-izumo, where the gods are understood to hold a final farewell banquet (直会, naōrai) before their departure.
events.prayer: The prayer at the heart of this entire sequence is for musubi (むすび) — the bonding force that underlies all meaningful human connection. Worshippers who come to Izumo during the Kamiari-zuki pray for good bonds of every kind to be established in their lives for the coming year: romantic bonds, bonds of friendship and community, bonds of opportunity and vocation, bonds of health between mind and body. Those who participate in the Enmusubi Taisai make a specific formal petition for good bonds (幸縁) to be woven into their destinies by the assembled gods under Ōkuninushi's direction. The prayer is understood not as a wish directed at an indifferent power but as a submission of one's hopes to the deliberative process of the divine assembly — trusting that the assembled gods, gathering with full knowledge of every human life in Japan, will weave the threads of connection as wisely as possible.

type.notes: The Kamimukae Shinji at Inasa-no-hama on the evening of the lunar 10th (the first night of the sequence) is the single most accessible and atmospheric event: arriving at the beach by 6:30 pm secures a position at the ritual space entrance, and the distribution of white paper wands at the gate permits you to join the procession from the beach to the shrine as a formal escort of the gods. This 20–30 minute ceremony — the sacred fire at the ocean's edge, the torchlit procession, the sound of court music in the November dark — is one of the most genuinely otherworldly ritual experiences in Japan. The Enmusubi Taisai (on the 15th and 17th lunar days) requires advance registration by postcard approximately one month prior (details announced on the official shrine website izumooyashiro.or.jp); with registration, participation in the formal prayer ceremony before the main sanctuary is possible. Without registration, visitors can still attend the shrine during the entire Kamiari-zuki period — the east and west Jūku-sha (神々の宿) stand open with their gods present, the Ryūja-gami is displayed for free veneration in the west corridor, and the atmosphere of the precinct is unlike any other time of year. The shrine explicitly asks that visitors remember this is not a tourist event but an active sacred assembly: no festival stalls, no entertainment, quiet and respectful conduct throughout. Plan to arrive on a weekday rather than weekend if possible — the first weekend of the Kamiari-sai routinely causes 2–3 hour traffic delays on the approach roads.

events.name: Imperial Grand Festival — The Three-Day Sacred Assembly (大祭礼, Daisairei / 例祭, Reisai)
events.time: May 13 (Eve Festival, 8 pm at the worship hall), May 14 (Reisai — Grand Annual Festival with Imperial envoy, 9 am Archery Purification + 10 am main ceremony), May 15 (Second Festival + Sacred Mikoshi Procession, noon), May 16 (Third Festival). This three-day cluster is collectively called the Daisairei (大祭礼).
events.origin: The present Daisairei descends directly from the ancient 三月会 (Sangatsu-e, the Third-Month Assembly), which was held on the 1st through 3rd days of the third lunar month and was described in historical records as 'the supreme festival of the San'in region, the foremost sacred rite of the nation' (山陰無双之節会、國中第一之神事ナリ). When Izumo Taisha was elevated to the status of Kanpei Taisha (官幣大社, a shrine receiving imperial offerings) by the Meiji government in 1871, the ancient Third-Month Assembly was inherited and reorganized into the current Daisairei, inaugurated in 1886 (Meiji 19). Like Izumo's participation in the Chokusha system — shrines receiving the Emperor's direct ceremonial attention — it joined a select group of fewer than 20 shrines nationwide, alongside Ise Jingū, the Kamo shrines, Kasuga Taisha, Katori Jingū, Kashima Jingū, Atsuta Jingū, and Hikawa Shrine. On May 14, the Emperor's envoy (勅使, chokuji) arrives bearing a lacquered box (唐櫃, karabitsu) containing the Emperor's silk offerings and a formal prayer document (御祭文, gosaimon). This is the moment at which Japan's imperial authority, tracing its lineage from Amaterasu, formally expresses reverence to the god who voluntarily ceded the visible world to that lineage — completing, in ritual form, the mythological covenant of the Kunitsukami annually.
events.meaning: The Daisairei enacts the primordial relationship between Ise and Izumo — between the sunlit world of imperial authority and the unseen world of divine fate — in the most formal institutional language available: the imperial envoy, representing Amaterasu's descendant the Emperor, comes to Ōkuninushi's dwelling to pay respect, present offerings, and read prayers. The worship protocol is different from every other day of the year: where standard Izumo worship uses four claps, the Reisai requires eight (八拍手, yashiode-te) — the number of infinity, a clapping that recognizes the god without limit. The sacred archery of the 的射祭 (Maoi-sai, Target-Shooting Ceremony) that precedes the main ceremony is not martial in intent but purificatory: the arrows released down the great pine approach are understood as piercing and dispersing the accumulated evil (邪物) that must be cleared before the gods can be approached in full ceremony.
events.ritual: The festival opens on the evening of May 13 at 8 pm with the Eve Festival in the worship hall. On May 14 at 9 am, the 的射祭 is performed at the approach path south of the Fourth Torii: priests in the special formal court robes (正服, seifuku) worn only on this one day of the year — a costume unchanged in form since antiquity — take positions in the pine-lined approach and fire two ritual arrows down the path. The arrows' role is purificatory rather than competitive: it is the act of release, not accuracy, that clears the sacred space. At 10 am the formal Reisai begins before the main sanctuary, the Imperial envoy presenting the silk offerings and reading the imperial prayer text while the full assembly of priests in ancient dress participates. On May 14 afternoon, the 田植舞神事 (Taue-mai Shinji, Rice-Planting Dance Ceremony) is performed at the east side of the worship hall — a ritual enactment of the rice planting cycle that connects the cosmic events of the festival to the agricultural calendar — followed at approximately 1 pm by the 流鏑馬神事 (Yabusame Shinji, mounted archery ceremony) along the great pine approach. On May 15 at noon, the 神輿渡御祭 (Mikoshi Togyo-sai, Sacred Portable Shrine Procession) brings the most visible and festive element of the festival: the mikoshi portable shrine is carried through the town of Taisha by the local parishioner community, accompanied by a 稚児行列 (chigo gyōretsu, procession of formally dressed children) and the sound of flute and drum. On May 16, the Three Festival (三之祭) and the Izumo Yashiki Thanksgiving Great Ceremony close the sequence. Throughout all three days, Izumo Kagura sacred dance is performed at the shrine, vendors occupy the approach, and exhibitions and sports events fill the surrounding town.
events.prayer: The Reisai prayer, spoken by priests on behalf of the Emperor and nation, thanks Ōkuninushi for his continuing governance of the invisible world — for the bonds of human life that he has maintained, the harvests he has permitted, the connections he has woven — and petitions for the continued exercise of his divine authority (神事の世界のご加護) over all that lies beyond human sight in the year ahead. Individual worshippers attending the Daisairei pray for the blessings that fall within Ōkuninushi's specific domain: good bonds of all kinds, health and healing, the prosperity of their households and businesses, and the safe continuation of their community.

type.notes: The Daisairei is the year's grandest and most visually complete festival at Izumo Taisha — a full three days of ceremony, procession, performance, and communal celebration. The most publicly accessible highlights are the 的射祭 on the morning of May 14 (the archery purification visible from the approach), the Yabusame mounted archery on May 14 afternoon (riders in full Heian costume galloping down the pine approach), and the Mikoshi Togyo-sai procession on May 15 (the most visually festive element, beginning at noon from the main sanctuary and processing through the town). The special eight-clap worship protocol during the Daisairei period is an experience available only on these days — remember that Izumo Taisha's standard four-clap practice is itself unique among Japan's major shrines, and the eight-clap version amplifies that uniqueness further. The pine-approach setting — a 200-meter corridor of ancient pines between the Second and Fourth torii — provides one of the most stately and ancient-feeling ceremonial spaces in Japan, especially when formally-robed priests and the Imperial envoy's party process through it.

events.name: Summer Cool-Taking Sacred Reed Ceremony (凉殿祭, Suzumidono-no-Matsuri) — also called the Makomo Shinji (真菰神事, Sacred Reed Ceremony)
events.time: June 1, from approximately 9 am at 'Izumo no Mori' (出雲の森), the sacred camphor grove east of the main precinct, continuing along the path to the Copper Torii (銅鳥居).
events.origin: The 凉殿祭 is one of the most intimate and least-known sacred rites in the Izumo Taisha calendar — and one of the most ancient in character, rooted in a specifically local legend about Ōkuninushi's habits. The shrine tradition holds that every year on June 1, as summer heat begins to build in the Izumo basin, Ōkuninushi changes into his summer garments and retreats to the grove of great camphor trees east of the main precinct — called 'Izumo no Mori' (出雲の森) — to take cool shade from the sun. The path he takes, from the grove to the sacred well of the Copper Torii (御手洗井, the Mitarashi-no-i), is the route he walked in divine nature, and it is this path that the ceremony reenacts. The use of makomo (真菰, Zizania latifolia, a water-grass of the sedge family) is fundamental: at Izumo Taisha, the sacred shimmenawa (注連縄) ropes within the inner sanctuaries are woven from makomo rather than the rice straw used at most other shrines — makomo is understood here as a plant of divine presence, its blade-like leaves understood to cut away evil and its hollow stem understood to receive divine spirit. The custom of the Kokuzo (出雲国造, the hereditary Grand Priest of the shrine, descended from the ancient Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko lineage) walking on the makomo rather than the earth reflects his status: as the hereditary intermediary between Ōkuninushi and the human world, the Kokuzo in ancient tradition was understood to be too sacred to touch ordinary ground — until the Meiji era, he moved exclusively by palanquin.
events.meaning: The 凉殿祭 reveals a side of Ōkuninushi almost invisible in the grand theological framework of the Kunitsukami and the Kamiari-sai: the god as a living presence with bodily rhythms, seasonal habits, and a relationship to the specific landscape of Izumo that is tender and domestic rather than cosmic and commanding. He changes clothes with the season; he seeks shade when it is hot; he walks a particular path through a grove that belongs to him. This is the sacred expressed not in the drama of divine assembly but in the quiet intimacy of a god at home in his chosen land. The makomo that the Kokuzo walks upon is simultaneously a protection for him (lifting the sacred feet from ordinary earth), a gift of divine power to the common people who receive it (having been touched by the Kokuzo's sanctified step), and a bridge between the Ōkuninushi's invisible world and the human world of seasonal bodies and mortal health.
events.ritual: On the morning of June 1, following the regular monthly ceremony at the main sanctuary, priests prepare the route: from the Izumo no Mori camphor grove eastward to the Mitarashi-no-i sacred well beside the Copper Torii, at equal intervals they heap small mounds of sand (立砂, tatesuna) from Inasa-no-hama beach, and upon this path they lay down fresh bright-green stalks of makomo one by one. An altar is erected before the great camphor tree in the grove, where offerings of shitogi-dango (粢団子, uncooked rice-flour dumplings specific to Izumo ritual tradition) and hitoyozake (醴酒, one-night sweet sake brewed overnight without fermentation) are placed beside the divine seat. The Kokuzo then advances in formal ceremony from the main precinct, holding the great sacred wand (大御幣), and walks — barefoot upon the makomo, not touching the earth — along the entire prepared path from the grove to the sacred well, where he pauses in silent prayer (黙祷祈念). When the ceremony is complete, the assembled worshippers rush forward to seize pieces of the makomo from the path. Those who carry pieces home and place them in the bath are believed to gain mubyō-sokusai (無病息災, protection from illness and calamity); those who place them in rice paddies or fields are believed to gain protection from crop-destroying insects (田の虫除け); those who place them in the household kamidana (神棚) are believed to receive the divine protection of Ōkuninushi for the household through the summer season. The makomo that remains is formed into small rings (真菰の輪, makomo-no-wa) placed at three points along the approach — at the Copper Torii, in front of the kagura hall, and at a third location — through which worshippers can pass (making the motions of the traditional chinowa-kuguri) to receive protection for the season. These rings remain in place until June 7.
events.prayer: Participants in the 凉殿祭 pray for protection from the illnesses and harm that gather in the transition from spring to summer — the season when, according to ancient Japanese understanding, the boundary between the world of the living and the dangerous forces of disease and spiritual pollution becomes most permeable. The prayer is specifically for mubyō-sokusai and for abundant harvests free from insect damage. More broadly, the act of receiving a piece of makomo walked upon by the Kokuzo is understood as receiving a fragment of Ōkuninushi's divine protection, transmitted through the hereditary channel of the ancient Izumo priestly lineage.

type.notes: The 凉殿祭 is almost completely unknown outside the Izumo local community and represents one of the most intimate and genuinely ancient ritual experiences available at any major shrine in Japan. There is no admission, no reservation, no performance to watch in the conventional sense — you simply arrive at the Izumo no Mori grove by 9:30 am and witness the Kokuzo's procession along the makomo-covered path. The atmosphere is quiet, participatory, and deeply local. The scramble for pieces of makomo as the ceremony concludes is good-natured and immediate — a direct physical exchange between the sacred and the ordinary. June 1 in Izumo is warm with early summer humidity, the camphor grove is dense and green, and the surrounding shrines and approach are uncrowded. For the visitor who has already experienced Izumo during the Kamiari-sai or the Daisairei, the 凉殿祭 reveals a completely different face of the shrine — not the cosmic gathering place but the intimate domestic world of a god at home.

events.name: Ancient Transmission Harvest Thanksgiving — The Night of the Living Fire (古伝新嘗祭, Kotden Niinamesai)
events.time: November 23 (the same day as the national public holiday 勤労感謝の日), from 7 pm. The ceremony is performed in near-complete darkness in the worship hall.
events.origin: The 古伝新嘗祭 is Izumo Taisha's most singular sacred rite — an act of such ancient character and so distinctive in form that the shrine prefixes it with 古伝 (Kotden, 'Ancient Transmission') to distinguish it entirely from the standard Niinamesai observed at shrines across Japan on the same date. The ceremonies at other shrines offer new grain to the gods and pray for thanksgiving. The 古伝新嘗祭 does something more: the Kokuzo himself — as the living intermediary between the human and divine worlds — partakes of the new grain alongside the gods in the ancient act of Aimae-no-gi (相嘗の儀, the Shared Tasting), eating the food offered to the gods in an act that was once understood as a direct channel of divine power into the physical body of the hereditary priest. Furthermore, the fire used to cook the sacred food is not ordinary fire — it is fire kindled by the sacred friction-starter (燧臼, hikiri-usu and 燧杵, hikiri-kine) received from Kumano Taisha (熊野大社) in Izumo, the shrine that holds the ancient title of 'originator of Japan's fire.' Every year, Izumo Taisha's Kokuzo receives the sacred fire-starter from Kumano Taisha at a formal exchange ceremony (鑽火祭, Sanka-sai) held at Kumano Taisha, and this borrowed fire is used to prepare the food of the 古伝新嘗祭 — a living chain of fire tradition that links the two most ancient shrines of Izumo in direct ritual cooperation. After the Aimae-no-gi, the Kokuzo writes the words 'New Harvest Festival Sacred Fire-Starter' (新嘗祭御燧臼) on the fire-starter with ink, then takes a small stone from the Manahoi (真名井, the sacred well of the precinct) and performs the Hagata-me-no-gi (歯固めの儀, Tooth-Hardening Ceremony) — pressing the stone to his teeth as an ancient act of praying for continued vitality and longevity. Finally, holding two small branches of sacred sakaki in both hands, he performs the Hyakuban-no-mai (百番の舞, the Hundred-Times Dance) — a solo sacred dance performed to ancient divine songs (神歌) in the near-darkness, by which he is believed to absorb the spiritual power (霊力, reiryoku) of the ancestral gods into his own body.
events.meaning: The 古伝新嘗祭 is the most intimate and cosmologically charged of all Izumo Taisha's rites. It is understood as a moment of literal divine recharging: as winter approaches, the spiritual power that flows from Ōkuninushi through the Kokuzo and outward to the human world is understood to diminish with the shortening of daylight. The 古伝新嘗祭 is the act that renews that power — the shared meal, the borrowed fire, the sacred dance, the tooth-hardening stone together constitute a ritual in which the ancient connection between the living human lineage of the Kokuzo and the divine lineage of Ōkuninushi is physically renewed for another year. The specific use of fire from Kumano Taisha adds a layer of inter-shrine sacredness: this is not merely Izumo Taisha performing a ceremony alone but the two great ancient shrines of Izumo completing a ritual partnership that encodes the ancient understanding of Japan's divine fire tradition. The official shrine record of this ceremony describes the Kokuzo's ultimate purpose: through the Aimae-no-gi and the Hyakuban-no-mai, 'the spiritual power of the ancestral gods, which has diminished with the coming of winter, is revived in the Kokuzo's body.'
events.ritual: At 7 pm, when the precinct has grown dark and still, the call of 'Ojare-mō!' (おじゃれもォ — a beautiful archaic Izumo dialect phrase meaning 'Please come forth!') rings from the worship hall into the night, and the Kokuzo and the assembled priests advance in procession by the light of dim oil lamps (燈明) alone — no electric light, no modern illumination. Inside the hall, only the faintest flicker of lamp oil lights the space. The Kokuzo presents the sacred offerings of new-harvest rice (御飯) cooked over the Kumano sacred fire, the hitoyozake sweet sake brewed overnight, to the four directions of heaven and earth — North, South, East, West — and to all the gods present. He then partakes of both offerings himself in the Aimae-no-gi. After inscribing the fire-starter, he retrieves the small stone from the sacred well and holds it to his teeth in the Hagata-me-no-gi. Finally, holding the sakaki branches, he performs the Hyakuban-no-mai in near-darkness, moving through the ancient dance steps to the words of sacred songs whose origins are immemorial. Simultaneously, a secondary ceremony — the Okama Shinji (御釜神事, Sacred Cauldron Ceremony) — is performed in the worship hall: the sacred cooking cauldron (御釜) brought from the Kama-sha (釜社, the Cauldron Shrine within the precinct) is carried to the hall, and priests circle it in a walking ceremony praying for the abundant harvest.
events.prayer: The prayer of the 古伝新嘗祭 is at once universal and intensely personal: universal in that it gives thanks for the harvest that has fed the nation and prays for the renewed abundance of the coming year; personal in that the Kokuzo's performance of the Aimae-no-gi and the Hyakuban-no-mai is specifically for his own renewal as the living channel between the gods and the human world — a prayer that his body, his lineage, and his sacred function will continue with undiminished vitality into the next year. For ordinary worshippers, visiting the shrine on November 23 is an act of pilgrimage on the night when this secret renewal takes place — knowing that in the darkness of the worship hall, the ancient priest is eating the gods' food and dancing their dance.

type.notes: The 古伝新嘗祭 is entirely closed — the ceremony in the darkened worship hall is conducted exclusively by the Kokuzo and the officiating priests, entirely beyond the sight of ordinary visitors. Its power as a pilgrimage event lies not in what can be seen but in what is known to be happening: arriving at Izumo Taisha on the evening of November 23, standing before the main sanctuary in the autumn night while the lights of the precinct burn low and the distant sound of the ceremony reaches the outer air, knowing that in that darkness the hereditary priest is performing an act of sacred renewal whose unbroken tradition reaches back to the Age of the Gods — this is pilgrimage in its most essential form. The precinct is open for ordinary worship until the late hours; November 23 is also the date of the 神在祭 and 縁結大祭 in some years (when the lunar calendar falls that way), making it potentially doubly significant. The connection to Kumano Taisha (easily visited from Izumo via a 30-minute drive) adds a dimension of inter-shrine pilgrimage: the Sanka-sai fire-exchange ceremony at Kumano Taisha, where Izumo Taisha's Kokuzo formally receives the sacred fire-starter, is itself a publicly visible ceremony of great beauty.

shrine: Ōmiwa Shrine (Miwa Shrine / 大神神社)
deities: Ōmononushi no Ōkami (大物主大神) — the Great Master of Things; sovereign deity of Mt. Miwa and the god of nation-building, medicine, agriculture, and sake brewing; understood at this shrine as a serpent deity who physically dwells within the mountain, capable of intimate contact with the human world and terrible wrath when his sacred form is violated; simultaneously the god who sends plague and the only power capable of stopping it

Ōnamuji no Kami (大己貴神) — another name for Ōkuninushi, the Great Land Master; enshrined here as a co-deity alongside Ōmononushi, with whom he shares a complex identity — the Nihon Shoki describes Ōmononushi as the gentle and wondrous soul (幸魂奇魂) of Ōnamuji himself, making the two deities aspects of the same divine continuum rather than entirely separate beings

Sukunabikona no Kami (少彦名神) — the Tiny Lord from Across the Sea; divine partner of Ōnamuji in the building of the Japanese land; together they established agriculture, medicine, sake brewing, and the arts of civilized life before Sukunabikona vanished across the sea to the eternal realm (常世), leaving Ōnamuji to complete the work alone

events.name: Spring Grand Festival (春の大神祭)
events.time: April 8–10 annually — eve ceremony (宵宮祭) on April 8, main festival (大神祭) on April 9, closing ceremony and Noh performance (後宴祭・後宴能) on April 10
events.origin: The Spring Grand Festival carries within it the memory of the most catastrophic moment in the early Japanese state — and the covenant that ended it. In the fifth year of Emperor Sujin's reign, a great epidemic swept the Yamato plain and the population collapsed. Fields were abandoned, families dissolved, and the young state teetered on the edge of ruin. In the seventh year, the god Ōmononushi no Ōkami himself appeared to the emperor in a dream and spoke directly: the plague was his doing, and it would not end until a man named Ōtataneko — his own divine descendant — was found and installed as his chief priest. Messengers were dispatched across the provinces, and Ōtataneko was found in the village of Chinu in Kawachi. He was brought before the emperor, the great rite was performed with him as officiating priest, and the plague ceased. The harvest returned. The people recovered. Emperor Sujin, overwhelmed with gratitude, ordered that the ceremony be repeated every year at the same season, on the Day of the Rabbit (卯の日, u-no-hi) in spring — and so it has been performed, without interruption, for over two thousand years. The festival was known for most of its history as the *U-no-Hi Shinji* (卯日神事, 'Rabbit Day Rite'), and when the Meiji calendar reform replaced the lunar calendar, the date was fixed at April 9 because that was the upper Rabbit Day in April of Meiji 6 — and it has remained April 9 ever since.
events.meaning: The Spring Grand Festival is not merely a commemoration of a past event — it is the annual renewal of the living covenant between Ōmononushi and the Japanese people. Each year, the god's agreement to protect the land from plague, to ensure the harvest, and to sustain the vitality of the nation is reaffirmed through the rite, and in return the people fulfill their half of the covenant: they come in their thousands, they offer the sacred sake brewed in his honor, they perform the ancient dance, and they carry his divine presence through the town in procession. Spiritually, the festival marks the moment when the god's power fully activates for the new agricultural and brewing season — his blessing descends into the rice paddies, the fermentation vats, and the human body all at once. There is also a dimension of atonement here: Ōmononushi is a god capable of genuine anger, and the annual ceremony is in part an acknowledgment that his divine will must be properly honored if the nation is to prosper. The presence of the young deity Ōtataneko-no-Mikoto (大直禰子命) — the divine grandson whose discovery ended the plague — is honored separately in the Wakamiya Jinkōsai procession on the afternoon of April 9, reminding the community that the covenant is also a lineage story: a story of the priestly Miwa clan whose blood runs from god to human and back.
events.ritual: The festival opens on the eve of April 8 with the *Yoimiya-sai* (宵宮祭), a quiet preparatory ceremony performed at dusk in the haiden to notify the god of the following day's rite. On the morning of April 9, approximately one thousand participants — shrine officials, parishioners, and representatives of affiliated organizations from across Japan — fill the haiden for the main ceremony. Sacred offerings are presented before the triple torii that frames Mt. Miwa beyond. Then four shrine maidens (*miko*) advance to the dance floor and perform the *Uma-zake Miwa no Mai* (うま酒みわの舞, 'Sweet Sake Miwa Dance') — an ancient sacred dance performed only twice a year at this shrine, in which the maidens hold branches of sacred cedar from Mt. Miwa and move through a slow, precise sequence of gestures as the ancient chant is sung: 'This sacred sake is not mine alone — it is the sake brewed by the Great Ōmononushi of Yamato — may it last long, may it last long.' In the afternoon of April 9, the divine spirit of Ōtataneko-no-Mikoto is ceremonially transferred from his sub-shrine into a portable mikoshi, and the *Wakamiya Jinkōsai* (若宮神幸祭) procession sets out through the town of Miwa — the chief priest on horseback, followed by priests in formal court attire, armor-clad warriors on horseback, children in ancient dress, and parishioners carrying the clan banners and sacred treasures of the various districts, totaling more than 250 participants in all. On April 10, after the closing ceremony (*kōen-sai*, 後宴祭), an outdoor Noh stage is erected in the shrine's sacred garden (*niwa*, 斎庭) and the *Kōen Nō* (後宴能) begins at noon — a three-hour performance of Noh and Kyōgen before the deity, including the play *Miwa* (三輪) attributed to Zeami himself, in which the god of this very mountain appears as a woman and reveals his divine nature, followed by the Kyōgen farce *Fuku no Kami* (福の神, 'God of Fortune'). Sacred rice cakes (*o-sonae mochi*) that have been offered to the deity are distributed to the audience in the afternoon.
events.prayer: National peace and the safety of all communities from epidemic and natural disaster — the original petition that caused this festival to be born. Abundant harvest for the coming agricultural year, that the rice paddies and the fields of the Yamato plain may be as productive as they were when the god first accepted his covenant with Emperor Sujin. The health and vitality of all who participate and all who cannot — the festival's original purpose was the healing of an entire civilization, and that universal scope of compassion remains present in every invocation. The blessing of all sake, miso, vinegar, and fermented foods to be produced in the coming year under the god's protection.

type.notes: The *Uma-zake Miwa no Mai* sacred dance on the morning of April 9 is visible to the public from the outer precinct area, though the inner ceremony is conducted by priests and invited participants. The real highlight for general visitors is the Wakamiya Jinkōsai procession on the afternoon of April 9, which moves through the streets of Miwa town and is entirely accessible — arrive by 12:30 PM to find a good position along the procession route near the shrine's second torii. The Kōen Nō performance on April 10 is free of charge, open to all visitors, and runs from noon until approximately 3:30 PM on the outdoor stage in the shrine garden — this is a genuinely rare opportunity to watch classical Noh performed outdoors in a sacred space without a ticket. Arrive by 11:45 AM for seating. The precinct is busy on April 9 but never overwhelmingly crowded by major shrine standards.

events.name: Flower-Calming Festival — Medicine Festival (鎮花祭 / 薬まつり)
events.time: April 18 annually, performed first at Ōmiwa Shrine then in procession to the adjacent Sai Shrine (狭井神社)
