events.origin: Long before modern germ theory, the people of ancient Japan observed a pattern that frightened them deeply: every year, when the cherry blossoms reached their peak and the petals began to scatter on the spring wind, the season of epidemic illness began. They understood this not as coincidence but as causation — the pestilence spirits (*ekishin*, 疫神) rode the falling petals outward from the mountains and spread themselves across the populated valleys below, alighting on the vulnerable and the unprotected alike. The only way to prevent this was to perform a rite powerful enough to calm the blossoms before they fell — to hold the petals still on the branches just a little longer, denying the disease spirits their vehicles of dispersal. This belief was so deeply embedded in the early Japanese state that when the *Taihō Code* (大宝律令) was promulgated in 701 CE — the great legal codification that organized every aspect of governance — it included a specific provision requiring this festival to be performed every year at Ōmiwa Shrine and Sai Shrine as a national obligation. The commentary *Ryō no Gige* (令義解) from the early Heian period explains the logic explicitly: 'When spring flowers scatter, the plague spirit disperses and causes epidemic; therefore the rite is performed at these two shrines to suppress and calm it.' The festival has been performed continuously under this legal mandate, and later as a matter of living tradition, for over thirteen hundred years.
events.meaning: The Chinka-sai illuminates something essential about how Ōmononushi no Ōkami is understood at this shrine: he is not merely a benevolent deity who grants petitions. He is a god of double nature — the one who sends the plague and the only one who can call it back. His fierce soul (*aramitama*, 荒魂) is enshrined at Sai Shrine precisely because that aspect of him — the aspect capable of devastating anger, of withdrawing his protection and unleashing pestilence — is too powerful to be held in the same space as the peaceable outer worship. The festival therefore moves in procession from the main shrine to Sai Shrine, formally addressing both aspects of the god on the same day: the accessible, generous Ōmononushi of the haiden, and the raw divine fury of the aramitama who must also be honored and appeased. In this sense, April 18 is the day when the full, unfiltered power of the god of Mt. Miwa is acknowledged — not just his blessings, but his capacity for harm, and the human community's vulnerable dependence on maintaining the right relationship with him. The festival also consecrates the bond between this ancient mountain deity and the medical tradition of Japan: the sacred herbs offered here — honeysuckle and lily bulb — are among the oldest medicines in the Japanese pharmacopoeia, and by presenting them to the god who governs both disease and healing, the ceremony makes every act of medicine a form of sacred relationship.
events.ritual: On the morning of April 18, the chief priest and assembled priests perform the main ceremony at Ōmiwa Shrine's haiden, offering sacred food and drink before the triple torii. The unique ritual offerings include *suikazura* (忍冬, Japanese honeysuckle — both the flower and the prepared medicinal extract) and *yurine* (百合根, lily bulbs ground into powder), presented in lacquerware vessels according to the ancient form preserved since the Nara period. Pharmaceutical companies, hospital associations, and medical practitioners from Nara, Osaka, Kyoto, and beyond attend in professional dress, presenting their own offerings of modern medicine and equipment to the deity — creating one of the most visually striking juxtapositions in Japanese religious life: thousand-year-old rites performed in the presence of representatives of contemporary biomedicine. After the main ceremony, the priests process in formal attire to the adjacent Sai Shrine — a short walk through the forested path that connects the two precincts — and perform the rite again before the aramitama of Ōmononushi, this time invoking his fierce aspect directly and petitioning for his restraint. After the ceremonies conclude, the shrine distributes three special seasonal items to worshippers: the *Chinka Gohei* (鎮花御幣, a paper wand of plague-warding) to be placed on the household altar or above the entrance; *Nindō-shu* (忍冬酒, sake brewed with Mt. Miwa honeysuckle and the sacred spring water of Sai Shrine's medicinal well); and *Nindō-ame* (忍冬飴, honeysuckle candy made from the same spring water) — all produced in limited quantities and distributed only on this day.
events.prayer: Protection from epidemic, contagion, and all forms of infectious illness for the coming season — the festival's ancient and unbroken purpose, which has never been more vividly understood than during the plague years of Japan's history. Healing of existing illness, particularly chronic conditions and inflammatory disease for which honeysuckle has long been prescribed in traditional Japanese medicine. Health and longevity for the entire household throughout the year, that no member will fall to the diseases that the scattering blossoms carry. For those in the medical and pharmaceutical professions, the blessing of skill, clear judgment, and efficacy in their work — that the god who controls all illness may guide their hands.

type.notes: The procession between Ōmiwa Shrine and Sai Shrine is fully visible and is the most distinctive visual element of the day — the formal procession of priests in court robes moving through the forested path between the two shrines takes place mid-morning and is accessible to any visitor standing along the path. The ceremony itself at both shrines is conducted semi-publicly; visitors may stand in the outer areas and observe the offerings. The post-ceremony distribution of honeysuckle sake, candy, and plague-warding wands begins shortly after the Sai Shrine ceremony concludes — typically late morning — and the honeysuckle sake in particular sells out within the first hour. Arrive before 10 AM and head directly to Sai Shrine after the main Ōmiwa ceremony to secure a place in the distribution line. The sacred medicinal spring at Sai Shrine (*kusuri-ido*, 薬井戸) is also open to visitors on this day; bring a small bottle to take the water home.

events.name: Sake Brewing Safety Festival (醸造安全祈願祭 / 酒まつり)
events.time: November 14 annually — the great cedar ball (*Ōsugi-dama*, 大杉玉) is hung one day earlier on November 13, making the 13th a secondary day of visual interest
events.origin: The bond between Ōmononushi no Ōkami and the art of sake brewing runs through two of the oldest sacred stories associated with this shrine. The first is embedded in the founding covenant of Emperor Sujin: when the plague was raging and the rites to appease the god were being prepared, Ōmononushi appeared in a second dream and commanded that a man named Ikuhi-no-Mikoto (活日命) be appointed as *sakabito* — sacred sake-brewer — and that divine sake be prepared for the offering. Ikuhi-no-Mikoto brewed a batch of extraordinary quality in a single night, by divine assistance, and when this *miki* (神酒, sacred sake) was offered to the god and shared among the people, the epidemic ceased. The song composed by Ikuhi-no-Mikoto in gratitude — 'This sacred sake is not mine alone — it is the sake brewed by the Great Ōmononushi of Yamato' — became the text of the *Uma-zake Miwa no Mai*, the sacred dance that is performed at this festival and at the Spring Grand Festival. The second thread is even older and more intimate: since the earliest times, the word for Miwa in the ancient poetic tradition had the pillow-word *uma-zake* (味酒, 'delicious sake') attached to it — meaning that by the time of the Man'yōshū poets in the 7th–8th centuries CE, the connection between the mountain and sacred brewing was so deep and so old it had entered the grammar of poetry itself. The custom of hanging a cedar-branch ball at the entrance of sake breweries to announce newly completed sake originated here: when the new cedar ball is hung at Ōmiwa Shrine each November, brewers across Japan take it as the signal — and the divine blessing — to begin their own season.
events.meaning: In the Shinto understanding of fermentation, the transformation of rice and water into sake is not chemistry but theology. The brewer does not make sake — the brewer provides the conditions, and the divine power of the kami completes the transformation. This is why Ōmononushi, who governs water, grain, the invisible forces of nature, and the subtle processes of growth and change, is the supreme patron of all fermented products: sake, miso, soy sauce, vinegar, and the full range of fermented foods that form the nutritional and cultural backbone of Japanese civilization. The November festival is the moment when this relationship between god and brewer is formally acknowledged and renewed: the brewer bows before the mountain, presents the fruits of the previous year's work, and asks for the same invisible divine participation in the new season that made last year's sake what it was. The replacement of the old cedar ball with a fresh one is the visible, outward sign of this renewal — the green of the new cedar against the brown of the old, the smell of fresh wood filling the precinct, the god's energy understood to be refreshed and ready.
events.ritual: On November 13, the great cedar ball is made fresh from branches of sacred cedar gathered from Mt. Miwa — an enormous sphere approximately 1.5 meters in diameter and weighing close to 200 kilograms — and hung from the eave of the haiden, replacing the previous year's dried and browned ball. The contrast between old and new, withered and vital, is stark and deliberate. On November 14, the main ceremony begins in the late morning as sake brewers, distillers, miso and soy sauce producers, vinegar makers, and representatives of fermentation-related industries from across Japan enter the haiden in formal dress and take their places before the deity. The chief priest recites the norito (祝詞, formal sacred address) petitioning Ōmononushi for safe and successful brewing throughout the coming season. Then the four shrine maidens advance and perform the *Uma-zake Miwa no Mai* — the same ancient cedar-branch dance performed at the Spring Grand Festival, now performed in honor of the brewing season specifically — singing the ancient song of Ikuhi-no-Mikoto as they move through the sacred cedar-scented air. Before the haiden, dozens of *komodaru* (こもだる, sake casks wrapped in traditional straw matting) have been stacked in a decorative pyramid as offerings, each representing a different brewery from across Japan. Individual sample bottles from hundreds of breweries nationwide line the offering tables. After the ceremony, the gathered sake is shared among participants in the *naorai* (直会, sacred communal meal) — meaning that the sake offered to the god flows back to the human world, completing the cycle.
events.prayer: Safe completion of the brewing season, that every vat of sake fermenting under Ōmononushi's protection will mature correctly and reach its full flavor without accident, contamination, or loss. Commercial prosperity and continued vitality for all breweries and fermentation producers who come to offer their devotion here — that the god who animated the first miraculous batch of divine sake in a single night will continue to be present in every fermentation that follows his name. The health and safety of all who work in the brewing industry throughout the season. And more broadly — the prayer that the ancient sacred relationship between this mountain, this water, this rice, and this god will never be forgotten, that the sake raised at every table in Japan will carry within it, invisibly, the blessing of the god of Mt. Miwa.

type.notes: The hanging of the new great cedar ball on November 13 is itself worth visiting — the 1.5-meter sphere of bright green cedar mounted against the weathered haiden facade is one of the most visually distinctive sights at any shrine in Japan, and the contrast with the old brown ball beside it makes the symbolism immediately legible even to visitors with no background in the tradition. On November 14, the ceremony inside the haiden is attended by industry participants but the stacking of sake casks before the haiden and the overall atmosphere of the precinct — fragrant with cedar and filled with brewers from across the country — is fully accessible to any visitor. Sake from breweries nationwide is typically available for informal tasting in the precinct area on this day, making it one of the most enjoyable and unpretentious festival experiences at the shrine. No reservation or ticket is required. The precinct is moderately busy but never uncomfortably crowded.

events.name: Sacred Mountain Ascent (三輪山登拝)
events.time: Year-round, daily; registration at Sai Shrine (狭井神社) from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM, with descent required by 4:00 PM. Closed during heavy rain, strong wind, major festival days, and certain sacred periods — check the official website before visiting.
events.origin: Mt. Miwa has not merely been associated with Ōmononushi no Ōkami since ancient times — it is understood to be his body. The mountain is not a place where the god lives; it is the god's physical form made present in the world. This understanding reaches back past the beginning of written records in Japan: it is why the shrine has never built a main hall, why the worshipper faces the mountain through the triple torii rather than a wooden building, and why the mountain itself was closed to all ordinary human entry for the entirety of Japanese history until the modern era — because to enter the mountain without proper preparation and authorization was to enter the body of the god without permission, an act of profound violation. The three ancient sacred rock formations (*iwakura*, 磐座) within the forest — the Okitsū Iwakura at the summit, the Nakatsū Iwakura on the mid-slope, and the Hetsū Iwakura near the base — are not symbols or markers of the divine; they are understood as the god's actual physical presence, the nodes of sacred power that have resided undisturbed in the mountain since the age before human memory. The opening of the mountain to lay pilgrims in the modern era was a carefully bounded concession to the desire of sincere worshippers to approach the god directly — but the conditions imposed on entry deliberately preserve as much of the mountain's ancient sacred integrity as possible.
events.meaning: To climb Mt. Miwa is not to visit a shrine, not to observe a ceremony, not to admire a landscape. It is to enter the body of a god. The pilgrim who ties the white sash at the foot of the trail and begins the ascent is understood — and understands themselves — to be physically moving inside the divine presence, with every step a deeper penetration into the sacred. The three iwakura encountered along the route are not destinations in the way that a shrine building is a destination; they are encounters — moments when the pilgrim stands in the immediate presence of the god's physical substance, separated from it only by the fence of respect. This form of spiritual encounter has no equivalent at any other shrine in Japan. There is no priest to mediate, no incense smoke to signal the threshold between human and divine, no architectural frame to tell the worshipper where the sacred begins and ends. There is only the mountain, the silence of old cedar and oak forest, the cold of the higher air, and the awareness that every photograph prohibited, every piece of food withheld, every word kept quiet is an act of respect toward the being whose flesh surrounds the path on all sides. For many Japanese pilgrims who make this ascent annually, it is described as the most direct spiritual experience of their lives — something that cannot be replicated by any other act of worship.
events.ritual: The pilgrim arrives at Sai Shrine (狭井神社) — the sub-shrine of Ōmononushi's fierce soul, located at the foot of the mountain trail — and registers with the shrine office, presenting a nominal sacred offering (初穂料, currently ¥300). The office attendant provides a white linen sash (*tasuki*, たすき) to be worn diagonally across the chest throughout the entire ascent and descent — a garment of purification, marking the wearer as a person who has formally entered the sacred boundary. The attendant also explains and requires acknowledgment of the rules: no photography under any circumstances, no eating or drinking inside the mountain, no loud voices, no deviation from the marked trail, no removal of any natural object from the mountain. The trail begins beyond a simple wooden gate and enters the forest immediately. The path is unimproved — compacted earth and exposed roots, rising steadily through cedar and oak, with the undergrowth dense and the canopy closing quickly overhead. The Hetsū Iwakura is encountered within the first third of the ascent — a large rock outcropping surrounded by a simple wooden fence, before which pilgrims bow and offer silent prayer. The Nakatsū Iwakura on the mid-slope is larger and more imposing; the sense of deepening sacred atmosphere is consistent with altitude. The Okitsū Iwakura at the summit is the innermost sanctuary, the highest concentration of the god's presence, approached with the greatest formality. On descent, the pilgrim returns to the Sai Shrine office and reports their safe return — a small but important act of closure, acknowledging that they have moved through the god's body and emerged safely, with gratitude.
events.prayer: The mountain ascent carries no fixed liturgy and no prescribed petition — the god's presence is understood to be so total inside the mountain that any sincere prayer is received without ceremony. That said, the ascent has deep historical associations with healing of serious illness, the recovery from personal catastrophe, the purification of accumulated misfortune (*kegare*, 穢れ) that has built up over years of ordinary life, and the seeking of direct guidance at life's most bewildering junctures. Many pilgrims who ascend regularly describe it not as a prayer for any specific thing but as a practice of presence — the act of returning to the god's body year after year, of being held within the mountain's silence, and of emerging renewed in a way that no ceremony performed at a distance can quite replicate.

type.notes: Registration closes at noon sharp without exception — if you arrive at Sai Shrine at 11:59 AM you will not be admitted. Allow at least 15 minutes before the deadline for the registration process, and at least 2.5 to 3 hours total for the ascent and descent. Wear proper footwear — the trail is steep, uneven, and can be slippery in any weather. Bring water, as drinking is not permitted on the mountain but you may drink at the trailhead before entering. Photography is absolutely prohibited inside the mountain boundary — this is not a cultural suggestion but a condition of entry, and it is enforced. The mountain is closed on the main days of the Spring Grand Festival (April 9) and on certain other sacred dates — check the official Ōmiwa Shrine website (oomiwa.or.jp) for the full closure calendar before planning your visit. Autumn (October to November) offers the most beautiful trail conditions and comfortable temperatures. The experience is profoundly unlike visiting any other shrine in Japan — visitors consistently describe the atmosphere inside the mountain as unlike anywhere else they have been.

deities: Ōmononushi no Kami (大物主神) — God of the sea, agriculture, medicine, and nation-building; enshrined here in the Sanuki-specific tradition as the deity who governed western Japan from a palace atop Zōzu-san when the surrounding plain was still an inland sea; his maritime sovereignty over the Seto Inland Sea makes him Japan's supreme guardian of sailors and all who travel by water

Emperor Sutoku (崇徳天皇) — The 75th Emperor of Japan, exiled to Sanuki after the Hōgen Rebellion of 1156, who died unreconciled in 1164 and was enshrined here the following year out of fear of his vengeful spirit; counted among Japan's three great vengeful spirits (三大怨霊) alongside Sugawara no Michizane and Taira no Masakado; revered in Sanuki as a symbol of those who bear unjust suffering with enduring dignity

events.name: Grand Festival (例大祭 / Reitaisai)
events.time: October 9–11 annually — eve ceremony (宵宮祭) on October 9, main festival on October 10, closing ceremony (後宴祭) on October 11
events.origin: The Grand Festival of Kotohira-gū carries within it the memory of the shrine's two founding moments — the age of gods and the age of men — and the entire three-day ceremony is structured as a re-enactment of the divine covenant between Ōmononushi and the human community that has maintained this mountain sanctuary for millennia. The ritual logic of the festival reaches back to the earliest traditions of Konpira worship, when the deity of Zōzu-san was understood to be not merely a recipient of prayer but an active participant in the life of the community below — a god who descended from the mountain to move among his people, inspect the harvest, and accept their gratitude in person. The *Otōnin-sama* (お頭人様) tradition at the heart of the festival — in which a designated human representative serves as the living vessel of the deity's presence during the procession — reflects an archaic belief that the god could inhabit a worthy human intermediary, making the festival not a symbolic representation of divine encounter but the encounter itself. The designation of the *Otōnin* is drawn from among the leading parishioner families of the Kotohira district, rotating annually through an established hereditary order — a system that has continued through the centuries of Buddhist governance, Meiji transformation, and into the present.
events.meaning: The Grand Festival is the annual moment when the god of the mountain descends to the town, not the reverse. Throughout the rest of the year, the pilgrimage is a human act — the worshipper climbs toward the divine. On October 10, the divine comes down. The procession moves from the main hall down through the stone-step precinct and into the streets of Kotohira-machi below, the divine presence carried not in an abstract architectural object but in the person of the *Otōnin-sama* — a living human being who has prepared through ritual purification to serve as the deity's earthly form for the duration of the festival. This inversion of the usual direction of sacred movement — god toward people rather than people toward god — gives the Grand Festival a quality of divine intimacy that the rest of the year's worship, conducted from a distance of 785 stone steps, does not have. It is also the moment when the relationship between the shrine and the maritime community is formally renewed: representatives of fishing cooperatives, shipping companies, the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force, and seafarers' associations from across the Seto Inland Sea and beyond come to present their gratitude and their petitions for the coming year, making October 10 the single day in the year when the shrine's character as Japan's supreme maritime guardian is most vividly present.
events.ritual: The festival opens on the evening of October 9 with the *Yoimiya-sai* (宵宮祭) — a preparatory nocturnal ceremony at the main hall in which the shrine maidens perform sacred dance by firelight and the deity is formally notified of the following day's events. The main festival on October 10 begins in the morning with the great formal ceremony at the main hall: sacred offerings are presented, priests in formal court attire recite the *norito*, and shrine maidens perform the sacred kagura before the deity. The ceremony's centerpiece follows: the *Otōnin-sama* — the designated parishioner representative, dressed in formal Heian-period court dress and carried in a palanquin — descends the stone steps in a grand formal procession of more than 200 participants including mounted priests, parishioners in period costume carrying the clan banners and sacred treasures of each district, armor-clad warriors, and children in ritual dress. The procession moves through the streets of Kotohira-machi in a stately circuit, accepting the formal greetings of the townspeople, before returning to the shrine for the evening ceremony. On October 11, the closing ceremony (*kōen-sai*) formally returns the divine presence to its place in the mountain and marks the end of the three-day festival with offerings of gratitude.
events.prayer: Safe voyages and the protection of all who work upon the sea — the petition that has brought sailors, fishermen, and maritime workers to this mountain for over a thousand years, and which the Grand Festival renews in the most formal and ceremonially complete manner of the entire year. Abundant harvest and agricultural prosperity for the farming communities of the Sanuki plain below. The health and prosperity of all parishioner families who have maintained the shrine through their ancestral service. And the broader prayer that the covenant established between Ōmononushi and the people of Sanuki — god and community holding each other in faithful relationship across the centuries — will continue unbroken into the future.

type.notes: The procession of the *Otōnin-sama* through the streets of Kotohira-machi on October 10 is fully visible to the public and is the most distinctive and visually impressive element of the festival — the sight of Heian-period court dress, mounted priests, and the palanquin of the divine representative moving through the narrow streets of the old pilgrimage town is genuinely unlike anything seen at most other shrines in Japan. The procession typically departs the shrine precinct in the late morning and moves through the town for several hours before returning. Position yourself along the main street of Kotohira-machi for the best view, or follow the procession up through the lower precinct. The ceremony at the main hall is conducted by priests and invited participants, but visitors may observe from the outer areas. Accommodation in Kotohira-machi on October 9–10 should be booked well in advance as the festival draws large numbers of visitors from across Shikoku and the Seto Inland Sea region.

events.name: Kemari Court Football Festival (蹴鞠祭)
events.time: April 10 annually, performed in the precinct as part of the spring festival sequence
events.origin: Kemari (蹴鞠) — the ancient Japanese court sport in which players in Heian-period formal dress keep a deerskin ball aloft through elegant, cooperative kicking — has been associated with Kotohira-gū since the medieval period as one of the divine offerings performed before Ōmononushi. The court sport itself was introduced to Japan from Tang-dynasty China in the 7th century and became the exclusive recreation of the Heian aristocracy, who practiced it in the gardens of the imperial palace as a form of ritualized play that was simultaneously an aesthetic performance, a spiritual offering, and a demonstration of aristocratic grace. Its association with this shrine — a maritime deity on a remote mountain in Shikoku — reflects the extraordinary breadth of devotion that Konpira commanded during its medieval and Edo-period zenith, when even the most refined and exclusive cultural forms of the capital found their way to this distant peak as offerings. The kemari tradition at Kotohira-gū was designated a Kagawa Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, preserving a practice that has largely vanished from everyday Japanese cultural life.
events.meaning: The offering of kemari before the deity is an act of profound aesthetic piety — the idea that the most beautiful and accomplished forms of human activity are the most fitting gifts to the divine. In the Heian worldview that gave birth to kemari as a ritual offering, the gods were not merely powerful — they were refined, they appreciated elegance, and they were most pleased by performances that demonstrated the highest development of human skill and grace. To play kemari before Ōmononushi is to offer him the accumulated cultural achievement of the imperial court — a tradition of practiced, cooperative, aesthetically perfect movement performed in his honor. There is also a maritime dimension to this offering at Kotohira specifically: the ball must never touch the ground, just as a vessel must never sink — the shared principle of keeping something precious continuously aloft, through skill, timing, and the cooperation of many hands, resonates with the shrine's core identity as the protector of those who keep their ships above the sea.
events.ritual: On the morning of April 10, participants dressed in complete Heian court attire — including the distinctive tall black *eboshi* hats, layered *kariginu* hunting robes in the traditional five seasonal colors, and the deer-hide boots of the classical kemari player — assemble in the open court area of the precinct. The deer-hide ball (*mari*, 鞠) is formally presented before the main hall as a sacred offering. The priests perform the introductory ceremony, after which the players take their positions in the four corners of the court beneath the designated canopy trees — traditionally cherry, willow, maple, and pine — each player responsible for one corner of the sacred playing space. The ball is put into play and the participants begin the cooperative sequence of elegant instep kicks, passing the ball in arcing trajectories from player to player with the aim of keeping it airborne indefinitely. The calls of *Ari* (アリ, 'there!'), *Ya* (ヤ, 'yes!'), and *Ōto* (オウト, a sound of encouragement) ring through the precinct as the ball rises and falls in the spring air. The performance lasts for a fixed period before the ball is formally returned to the altar as the offering it has been throughout — not a game that was played, but a prayer that was danced.
events.prayer: The blessing of all cooperative endeavors — the implicit prayer of kemari, in which no single player can succeed without the skill and responsiveness of all the others, mirrors the prayer of every vessel crew that nothing may be dropped, nothing lost, and that all hands may work together well enough to bring everyone home. Artistic accomplishment and the mastery of refined skills. The preservation of beautiful and ancient things in a world that changes quickly. And the simple, repeated human prayer that what is precious and aloft may remain so — that the ball may not fall, the ship may not sink, and the grace of the moment may be extended just a little longer.

type.notes: The kemari performance on April 10 is fully open to the public and is one of the most visually distinctive and culturally unusual things a visitor can witness at any shrine in Japan — the complete Heian court dress, the unhurried elegance of the kicks, and the formal calls of the players create an atmosphere of having stepped entirely out of the present. The performance takes place in the open-air court area of the precinct and requires no ticket. April 10 also coincides with the sakura season at the shrine, when the Sakura-bama promenade is typically in bloom, making it one of the most beautiful and atmospheric days of the entire year to visit. Arrive by 9:30 AM to find a position near the performance area before the crowd fills in. The precinct is busy but the kemari itself draws a contained audience — there is usually room to watch comfortably.

events.name: Shikoku Konpira Grand Kabuki (四国こんぴら歌舞伎大芝居)
events.time: Late April to early May annually (specific dates vary by year — typically a 10-day run in late April)
events.origin: The theater building known as Kin Maroza (旧金毘羅大芝居 / 金丸座) was built in 1836 on the pilgrimage approach road to Kotohira-gū and is the oldest surviving kabuki theater in Japan. It was built during the height of the Konpira pilgrimage boom, when the combination of religious devotion, commercial activity, and Edo-period popular culture had transformed the approach to the shrine into one of the most vibrant entertainment districts outside of Osaka and Edo. Kabuki performers — whose work was officially designated as a form of sacred entertainment offered to the gods — had a natural home in the shadow of the shrine, and the *Kin Maroza* was constructed to give permanent form to what had already been a thriving tradition of theatrical performance at this pilgrimage destination. The theater fell into disuse as the Meiji period and its aftermath reshaped Japanese entertainment, and the building was eventually designated a National Important Cultural Property in 1970. The annual Grand Kabuki performance was revived in 1985 and has been held every spring since, bringing professional kabuki troupes — including performers from the great Kabuki families — to perform in the original Edo-period theatrical space.
events.meaning: The Konpira Kabuki is a living encounter with Edo-period performance culture in the only physical space in Japan where that culture was originally housed and has never been replaced. Unlike modern kabuki theaters, which are purpose-built concert halls with electric lighting, contemporary acoustics, and invisible stagecraft, the Kin Maroza retains its original rotating stage (*mawari butai*, 廻り舞台), trapdoors (*seri*, 迫り), and the *hanamichi* walkway through the audience — all operated by human power, as they were in 1836. The oil-lamp-and-candle atmosphere of the original performances cannot be fully recreated, but the physical space carries within it the memory of two centuries of performance and devotion, and the experience of watching kabuki in this intimate wooden theater — where every creak of the stage and every breath of the audience is part of the performance — is categorically different from watching it anywhere else. The revival of the performance as an annual event connected to the shrine also restores the original religious dimension: kabuki at Konpira was not secular entertainment that happened to take place near a shrine — it was a form of sacred offering, the accumulated beauty of human performance given to the god of the mountain.
events.ritual: The Kin Maroza is a compact wooden theater seating approximately 400 people on tatami floor seating (*sajiki*, 桟敷) and gallery benches, arranged around the thrust stage in the intimate configuration of classical Edo-period theater architecture. Audience members remove their shoes and sit on cushions on the tatami floor or in the wooden gallery above, at eye level with or slightly below the stage — a proximity to the performers that modern theaters, with their raised stages and distant seats, cannot replicate. The program typically presents two or three classical kabuki plays or dance pieces per performance, selected to represent the full range of the form: dramatic pieces (*jidaimono*, 時代物) depicting historical or legendary scenes, domestic pieces (*sewamono*, 世話物) depicting the lives of Edo commoners, and pure dance (*shosagoto*, 所作事) pieces in which the performer's physical movement is the entire content of the work. The rotating stage turns by human power from below; trapdoors deliver and remove characters; the hanamichi runway brings the most important moments of the performance directly through the center of the audience. Everything is visible, everything is craft, and the distance between the formal magic of the stage and the wooden reality of the building is small enough to cross with a single breath.
events.prayer: The prayer implicit in attending the Konpira Kabuki is not a petition but a participation — the act of being present in this ancient wooden space, watching human beings perform at the limit of their physical and artistic training, is itself a form of gratitude to the god who for two centuries has received this offering. For practitioners and devotees of the performing arts, the Konpira Kabuki carries the specific prayer that the tradition will continue — that the skills, the stories, and the physical buildings that house them will survive the pressures of modernity and remain alive for the people who come after. For ordinary visitors, it carries the simpler prayer of all great performance: that for the duration of the play, the ordinary world will fall away and something essential and true about human experience will briefly become visible.

type.notes: Tickets for the Shikoku Konpira Grand Kabuki are sold through the theater's official ticketing system and typically go on sale several months before the performance dates — demand is high and tickets, particularly for weekend performances, sell out quickly. The performance runs approximately 3.5 to 4.5 hours including intermissions. Tatami floor seating requires the ability to sit on the floor for extended periods; cushions are provided but the seating is not universally comfortable for Western visitors — the gallery bench seats offer a reasonable alternative. The theater has no air conditioning and in late April can be warm; dress in light layers. The Kin Maroza is a 5-minute walk from the base of the Kotohira-gū stone steps, making it straightforward to combine a morning shrine visit with an afternoon performance. Even outside of the performance season, the theater building itself is open to visitors as a heritage site, and the experience of simply walking through the empty Edo-period performance space — touching the worn wood of the stage, looking up at the lantern brackets, understanding the mechanics of the rotating stage — is worthwhile in its own right.

events.name: The 785-Step Pilgrimage Ascent (参道石段参拝)
events.time: Year-round, daily. Main hall accessible from approximately 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM (seasonal variation). Inner sanctuary (奥社 / 厳魂神社) closes earlier — confirm on official website.
events.origin: The stone-step approach to Kotohira-gū is not merely a path to a shrine — it is the pilgrimage itself. In the Edo period, when Konpira-san was one of the two most popular pilgrimage destinations in Japan alongside Ise, the act of climbing from the foot of the mountain to the main hall represented the entire purpose of the journey. Pilgrims had traveled for weeks from across the country — from Edo, from Osaka, from the northern provinces — enduring the roads, the weather, and the expense of the journey specifically in order to make this climb. The stone steps, with their attendant sub-shrines, cultural landmarks, and the physical demands of the ascent itself, were understood as the structure through which the ordinary world was left behind and the sacred was approached. Pilgrims who were too elderly or infirm to climb were carried in palanquins (*kago*) by professional bearers who operated the approach commercially well into the modern era. The tradition of the *Konpira Inu* — proxy-pilgrim dogs sent in their owner's place when the journey was impossible — speaks to how deeply the physical act of climbing these specific steps was understood as the irreplaceable center of the devotional experience. The steps exist in their current form as a result of centuries of accumulated piety: each stone was paid for by a donor, carved by a craftsman, and placed by laborers who understood themselves to be building a sacred road.
events.meaning: The ascent of the 785 steps to the main hall — and for the most devoted, the further 583 steps to the inner sanctuary at 1,368 total — is an act of physical devotion that transforms the body into the prayer. In Japanese pilgrimage culture, the effort of the journey is not incidental to its spiritual value — it is the spiritual value. Each step upward is an increment of surrender: of physical comfort, of the ease of the ordinary world, of the ego that prefers to remain at the bottom. The famous saying associated with Konpira pilgrimage — *Konpira-san ni mairu to, nembutsu ga iranai* ('If you visit Konpira-san, you have no need of prayers for the dead') — reflects the belief that the merit earned by completing the ascent was so substantial that it could satisfy a lifetime's spiritual obligations. The view from the main hall terrace at 251 meters — the Sanuki plain spread below, the Inland Sea visible in the distance, the bridges and the mountains of four prefectures on a clear day — is not merely a scenic reward but the visual confirmation of what the ascent was: the god's perspective, seen from the god's mountain, briefly available to those who made the climb.
events.ritual: The pilgrimage ascent begins at the stone base of the approach road (*sandō*) in the town below, where the dense commerce of souvenir shops and udon restaurants gives way to the first stone steps. At the 365th step, the great *Ōmon* gate — a two-story structure donated by Matsudaira Yorishige, the first lord of Takamatsu domain and elder brother of Mito's Tokugawa Mitsukuni — marks the boundary between the secular town and the sacred precinct. Beyond the gate, five white-umbrella candy vendors — the *Goninbyakusho* (五人百姓) — sell their amber-colored *Kamiyo ame* (加美代飴) from beneath enormous white umbrellas, the only commercial vendors permitted within the sacred grounds since the Kamakura period. The candy, shaped like a fan, is eaten by breaking it with a small wooden hammer and sharing the pieces — the name *Kamiyo* (神代, 'age of the gods') suggests that sharing the candy distributes divine blessing among those who receive it. Past the candy vendors, the *Sakura-baba* promenade leads to the *Shoin* complex with its Maruyama Ōkyo fusuma paintings. The *Asahi-no-Yashiro* — the elaborately carved two-story shrine building whose construction spanned roughly forty years — marks the final approach. The last section of the ascent, the *Gozen Shidan* (御前四段坂), is the steepest: four short, nearly vertical flights that deliver the pilgrim directly to the main hall terrace. Those who continue to the inner sanctuary climb an additional 583 steps through increasingly dense forest to the Izutama Jinja, where the carved tengu face watches from the cliff face and the *Tengu Omamori* (天狗御守) — available only at this altitude — awaits.
events.prayer: Maritime safety for all who travel or work upon the sea, the prayer that has drawn sailors and fishermen to this mountain for over a millennium and that remains as live today as it was in the Edo-period pilgrimage boom. Safe completion of any difficult journey — the metaphor of the ascent itself, completed step by step, translates directly into prayer for any undertaking that requires sustained effort and courage to complete. Health and longevity, for oneself and for those one loves. Business success and prosperity, honoring the Edo-period merchant tradition that made Konpira-san famous nationwide. And the private, untranslatable prayer that each pilgrim carries up the steps alone — the one that required the climb to say properly.

type.notes: The ascent to the main hall (785 steps) takes approximately 40 to 60 minutes at a moderate pace for an average fit adult — plan for longer if you stop at the Shoin or other points of interest along the way. The inner sanctuary (1,368 steps total) adds another 40 to 50 minutes. Wear proper footwear — the stone steps are worn smooth in places and can be slippery in wet weather. Walking sticks are available for rent or purchase at shops near the base. The ascent is genuinely strenuous in summer heat — the steps face east and receive full morning sun; arrive before 9 AM or after 4 PM in July and August. The *Shoin* (書院) with the Maruyama Ōkyo and Itō Jakuchū paintings requires a separate entrance fee and is open during regular visiting hours — it is one of the most remarkable collections of Edo-period screen painting accessible to the general public at any shrine in Japan and should not be missed. The *Goninbyakusho* candy vendors inside the gate accept cash only and sell out by early afternoon on busy days. Dogs are formally permitted in the precinct — uniquely among major shrines — in honor of the *Konpira Inu* tradition, but must not enter buildings.
